topics.title: Power BJT Drive Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL607 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Power BJT Drive Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Power BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor) Drive Circuit** is an interface network designed to deliver the precise base current ($I_B$) and base-emitter voltage ($V_{BE}$) required to switch a power BJT between its **cut-off region** (OFF state) and **saturation region** (ON state) in high-power switching applications. Unlike small-signal BJTs that can be driven directly by logic gates, power BJTs (such as the BUX series, MJ series, or Darlington power transistors) require substantial base currents — often ranging from **0.5 A to 5 A** — to achieve full saturation and maintain low collector-emitter saturation voltage ($V_{CE(sat)}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The drive circuit is *not* a passive component. It is an active switching controller that optimizes three critical parameters simultaneously: **(1)** turn-ON time ($t_{on}$), **(2)** storage time ($t_s$), and **(3)** turn-OFF time ($t_{off}$). Improper drive design leads to **secondary breakdown** and catastrophic device failure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a heavy industrial **warehouse sliding door** weighing several hundred kilograms. The door cannot be pushed open by hand — it needs an electric motor. However, simply *turning the motor ON* will slam the door violently, damaging the rollers. Instead, a **smart controller** is used:

- It first delivers **high torque (strong push)** to break the inertia and start the door moving (**= initial overdrive base current $I_{B1}$**).
- Once the door is sliding smoothly, the controller **reduces the torque** to a level just enough to keep it moving at constant speed (**= continuous base current $I_{B2}$** for maintaining saturation).
- To stop the door, the motor applies a **reverse braking force** to halt the door quickly without damage (**= reverse base bias $V_{BB}$ for fast turn-off**).

This "smart motor controller" is exactly analogous to a **BJT base drive circuit**. Without it, the power BJT would either fail to saturate (overheating) or switch too slowly (excessive switching losses).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A *logic-level* signal (0–5 V, a few mA) is fundamentally incapable of driving a power BJT that handles **100 A, 600 V** loads. The drive circuit performs the necessary **signal amplification + level translation + isolation**.

### 1.3 Critical Physical Parameters (KTU 2024 Emphasis)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Continuous Collector Current | $I_C$ | **1 A to 500 A** | Load current capability |
| Base Drive Current | $I_B$ | **0.5 A to 5 A** | Required to saturate the BJT |
| DC Current Gain | $h_{FE}$ ($\beta_{DC}$) | **5 to 75** (power BJTs are low-$\beta$!) | Determines base current demand |
| Collector-Emitter Saturation Voltage | $V_{CE(sat)}$ | **0.5 V to 2.5 V** | Conduction loss driver |
| Base-Emitter ON Voltage | $V_{BE(ON)}$ | **0.7 V to 2.5 V** | Threshold for turn-on |
| Switching Frequency | $f_{sw}$ | **1 kHz to 50 kHz** | Application range |
| Storage Time | $t_s$ | **1 $\mu$s to 10 $\mu$s** | Limits maximum frequency |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Students often confuse small-signal BJT $\beta$ values (200–400) with power BJT $\beta$ values (5–75). Always consult the **datasheet** of the specific power transistor under test.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why a Special Drive Circuit is Mandatory for Power BJTs

Power BJTs suffer from three intrinsic challenges that small-signal BJTs do not:

1. **Low Current Gain ($\beta$):** A power BJT handling 50 A collector current with $\beta = 10$ requires a base current of $5\,\text{A}$. Logic outputs cannot provide this.
2. **Long Storage Time ($t_s$):** The base region is heavily doped and wide, accumulating a large **minority carrier charge** $Q_B$ that must be removed before turn-off.
3. **Narrow Safe Operating Area (SOA):** During the switching transient, simultaneous high voltage and high current can exceed the **Reverse Bias SOA (RBSOA)** and cause **secondary breakdown**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core 'Why':** Drive circuits solve these three problems by (1) providing ample base current, (2) actively extracting stored charge, and (3) controlling the $dv/dt$ and $di/dt$ during transitions.

### 2.2 Base Drive Requirements — The Two-State Logic

A complete base drive waveform has **three distinct phases**:

| Phase | Drive Action | BJT Region Transition | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Turn-ON** | Inject **high forward base current** $I_{B1} = (2 \text{ to } 3) \cdot I_{B(\text{min})}$ | Cut-off → Active → Saturation | Reduce turn-ON delay + initial turn-ON time |
| **ON-State** | Reduce to **steady base current** $I_{B2}$ | Deep Saturation | Minimize $V_{CE(sat)}$ while reducing drive losses |
| **Turn-OFF** | Apply **reverse base voltage** $V_{BB} \approx -V_{BE}$ to **extract stored charge** | Saturation → Active → Cut-off | Eliminate storage time $t_s$ |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimum Base Current: } I_{B(\text{min})} &= \frac{I_{C(\text{max})}}{h_{FE(\text{min})}} \\[6pt]
\text{Overdrive Factor: } ODF &= \frac{I_{B1}}{I_{B(\text{min})}} \quad \text{typically } 2 \text{ to } 3 \\[6pt]
\text{Conduction Loss: } P_{cond} &= V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C \cdot D \\[6pt]
\text{Storage Time (Baker clamp mitigates): } t_s &= \tau_s \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{I_{B2} + I_{C}/\beta_F}\right) \\[6pt]
\text{Total Switching Loss: } P_{sw} &= \frac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_C (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_{sw} \\[6pt]
\text{Base Drive Power: } P_{drive} &= V_{BE} \cdot I_B \cdot D
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All absolute-value expressions in the cheat sheet use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ notation. In the exam answer sheet, use $\lvert V_{CE} \rvert$ rather than the pipe symbol `|` to avoid markdown rendering issues.

### 2.4 Practical Significance in Modern Engineering

- **Motor Drives (VFDs):** BJT/IGBT inverter legs require precisely synchronized complementary drive signals with **dead-time insertion**.
- **Switch-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS):** Push-pull and half-bridge topologies use base drive transformers for **galvanic isolation** between control and power stages.
- **UPS Systems and Welding Inverters:** Demand high-frequency operation where low $t_s$ and $t_f$ directly translate to higher efficiency.
- **Automotive Ignition Systems:** Darlington power BJTs are driven by high-$\beta$ booster stages in **ignition coil driver modules**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative 1: Calculating the Required Base Current

**Problem Context:** A power BJT (e.g., **2N3055**) is used in a switching regulator. The maximum collector current is $I_C = 20\,\text{A}$, and from the datasheet, $h_{FE(\text{min})} = 10$ at $I_C = 20\,\text{A}$, $V_{CE} = 4\,\text{V}$. The designer has selected an **overdrive factor of 2.5** to ensure fast turn-ON. Determine the required base current magnitudes and the supply voltage for the drive circuit.

**Step 1 — Compute the minimum theoretical base current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{B(\text{min})} &= \frac{I_{C(\text{max})}}{h_{FE(\text{min})}} \\[4pt]
I_{B(\text{min})} &= \frac{20\,\text{A}}{10} \\[4pt]
I_{B(\text{min})} &= 2.0\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** This is the *minimum* current that will keep the BJT on the *edge* of saturation. Operating here leaves no margin for temperature variation (since $\beta$ drops with temperature).

**Step 2 — Apply the overdrive factor (ODF) to compute $I_{B1}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{B1} &= ODF \times I_{B(\text{min})} \\[4pt]
I_{B1} &= 2.5 \times 2.0\,\text{A} \\[4pt]
I_{B1} &= 5.0\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** During turn-ON, the BJT must rapidly fill its base region with minority carriers. A higher $I_{B1}$ accelerates this and reduces $t_{on}$, but increases drive power loss.

**Step 3 — Compute the steady-state base current $I_{B2}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{B2} &= 1.5 \times I_{B(\text{min})} \\[4pt]
I_{B2} &= 1.5 \times 2.0\,\text{A} \\[4pt]
I_{B2} &= 3.0\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** A factor of 1.5 is the standard design rule — enough to maintain saturation under load transients, while saving $\sim 40\%$ of the drive power compared to continuous $I_{B1}$.

**Step 4 — Design the drive supply voltage $V_{BB}$ (turn-ON):**

Assuming a current-limiting base resistor $R_B$ and using $V_{BE(ON)} = 1.5\,\text{V}$ (typical for power Darlington):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_B &= \frac{V_{BB} - V_{BE(ON)}}{I_{B1}} \\[4pt]
\text{Choose } V_{BB} &= 12\,\text{V (standard logic rail)}\\[4pt]
R_B &= \frac{12 - 1.5}{5.0} = 2.1\,\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivative 2: Storage Time Reduction with Reverse Bias

**Problem Context:** A power BJT has a storage time constant $\tau_s = 2\,\mu\text{s}$, $I_{B1} = 5.0\,\text{A}$, $I_{B2} = 3.0\,\text{A}$, $\beta_F = 10$, $I_C = 20\,\text{A}$. Compute the storage time $t_s$ and then determine the effect of applying a reverse bias of magnitude $I_{BR} = 2.0\,\text{A}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $t_s$ with the natural turn-off (no reverse bias):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_s &= \tau_s \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{I_{B2} + I_C / \beta_F}\right) \\[4pt]
\frac{I_C}{\beta_F} &= \frac{20}{10} = 2.0\,\text{A} \\[4pt]
t_s &= 2\,\mu\text{s} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{5.0 + 3.0}{3.0 + 2.0}\right) \\[4pt]
t_s &= 2\,\mu\text{s} \cdot \ln(1.6) \\[4pt]
t_s &= 2\,\mu\text{s} \cdot 0.4700 \\[4pt]
t_s &= 0.94\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $t_s$ with reverse base current $I_{BR} = 2.0\,\text{A}$ applied:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_s' &= \tau_s \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2} + I_{BR}}{I_{B2} + I_C/\beta_F + I_{BR}}\right) \\[4pt]
t_s' &= 2\,\mu\text{s} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{5.0 + 3.0 + 2.0}{3.0 + 2.0 + 2.0}\right) \\[4pt]
t_s' &= 2\,\mu\text{s} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{10.0}{7.0}\right) \\[4pt]
t_s' &= 2\,\mu\text{s} \cdot \ln(1.4286) \\[4pt]
t_s' &= 2\,\mu\text{s} \cdot 0.3567 \\[4pt]
t_s' &= 0.713\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the percentage reduction in storage time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_s\,\% &= \frac{t_s - t_s'}{t_s} \times 100 \\[4pt]
\Delta t_s\,\% &= \frac{0.94 - 0.713}{0.94} \times 100 \\[4pt]
\Delta t_s\,\% &= 24.1\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even a modest reverse base current of **2 A** reduces storage time by **~24%**. In high-frequency designs (≥ 20 kHz), this directly translates to a **24% reduction in turn-OFF switching losses**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of BJT Drive Circuit Topologies

| Drive Topology | Complexity | Turn-ON Speed | Storage Time $t_s$ | Isolation | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple Resistor Drive** | Very Low | Slow | Long | None | Low-frequency DC switches |
| **Baker Clamp Drive** | Medium | Fast | Very Short | Optional | Inductive load switching |
| **Totem-Pole (Complementary) Drive** | Medium | Fast | Short | Optional | Low-power motor drives |
| **Transformer-Coupled Drive** | High | Fast | Short | **Galvanic** | High-voltage inverters, SMPS |
| **Darlington Driver (ULN2003)** | Low | Medium | Long | Optional | Relay/load driver arrays |
| **Proportional Base Drive (Anti-saturation)** | High | Very Fast | Very Short | Optional | High-frequency SMPS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Concept:** The **Baker Clamp** uses two diodes (D1, D2) to clamp $V_{CE}$ to a value just above $V_{CE(sat)}$, preventing *deep* saturation. This reduces stored charge by **~70%** and drastically lowers $t_s$, at the cost of slightly higher conduction loss (higher $V_{CE}$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture of a Generic Power BJT Drive System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Logic Control Signal PWM] --> B[Isolation Barrier Optocoupler or Pulse Transformer]
    B --> C[Pre-Driver Stage TTL to Level Shifter]
    C --> D[Totem Pole Driver Stage Push Pull Amplifier]
    D --> E[Base Drive Network R sub B and C sub B Snubber]
    E --> F[Power BJT Main Switching Device]
    F --> G[Inductive Load Motor or Coil]
    F --> H[Reverse Bias Turn OFF Network V sub BB]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style C fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style D fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b

    style F fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828

    style H fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow of the Three-Phase Drive Sequence

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[State 1: Turn ON Phase] -->|Inject I sub B1 = 2 to 3 times I sub B min| B[State 2: ON State Phase]
    B -->|Reduce to I sub B2 = 1.5 times I sub B min| C[State 3: Turn OFF Phase]
    C -->|Apply reverse base current I sub BR| D[State 4: Cut OFF State]
    D -->|Hold V sub BE less than 0.5 V| A

    style A fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style B fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style C fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style D fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of a Baker Clamp Drive

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Drive Signal V sub in] --> B[Base Resistor R sub B]
    B --> C[Base of Power BJT Q1]
    A --> D[Diode D1 Anode to Collector]
    D --> E[Diode D1 Cathode to Base]
    C --> E
    E --> F[V sub CE Clamped at V sub BE of D2 + V sub BE of Q1]
    F --> G[Power BJT Q1 Operating in Quasi Saturation]
    G --> H[Reduced Stored Charge and Short t sub s]

    style A fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style B fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c

    style F fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92
    style G fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40
    style H fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Why is a special base drive circuit required for a power BJT, whereas a small-signal BJT can be driven directly from a logic gate?*

**Model Answer:**
A power BJT handles large collector currents (often tens to hundreds of amperes) but has a **low DC current gain $h_{FE}$** (typically 5 to 75). For example, a BJT with $I_C = 50\,\text{A}$ and $h_{FE} = 10$ requires a base current of **5 A**, far exceeding what a logic gate (a few mA) can supply. Additionally, power BJTs exhibit long **storage time $t_s$** due to large stored base charge, requiring drive circuits that actively extract this charge via a **reverse base bias**. Small-signal BJTs have high $\beta$ (>200) and negligible stored charge, making direct logic-level drive feasible.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define storage time $t_s$ in a power BJT and state two techniques to reduce it.*

**Model Answer:**
**Storage time $t_s$** is the interval between the application of the turn-off base signal and the start of the collector current fall. It is the time required to remove the **excess minority carrier charge** stored in the base region during the ON state.

**Two techniques to reduce $t_s$:**
1. **Baker Clamp (Anti-saturation):** Prevents deep saturation by clamping $V_{CE}$ to a higher value, reducing stored charge.
2. **Reverse Base Bias Turn-off:** Applying a negative $V_{BB}$ extracts stored charge more rapidly through a reverse base current $I_{BR}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the operation of a **totem-pole base drive circuit** for a power BJT. Discuss the role of the complementary transistors $Q_1$ and $Q_2$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A power BJT has $I_C = 40\,\text{A}$, $h_{FE(\text{min})} = 8$, and $V_{CE(sat)} = 1.2\,\text{V}$ at full load. The drive circuit provides an overdrive factor of **2** during turn-ON and a steady-state factor of **1.2**. Calculate the base currents $I_{B1}$ and $I_{B2}$, and the conduction loss at duty cycle $D = 0.6$.

**Model Solution for (a):**

A **totem-pole drive** uses two complementary BJTs (one NPN, one PNP) stacked in series between the positive drive rail $+V_{BB}$ and the negative rail $-V_{BB}$ (or ground). The base of the power BJT is connected to the junction between the two complementary transistors.

**Operating Sequence:**
- When the input logic is **HIGH** (1), the upper transistor $Q_1$ (NPN) is turned ON and the lower transistor $Q_2$ (PNP) is turned OFF. Current flows from $+V_{BB}$ through $Q_1$ to the base of the power BJT, providing the **forward base drive current $I_{B1}$**.
- When the input logic is **LOW** (0), $Q_1$ is OFF and $Q_2$ is ON. The base of the power BJT is now connected to $-V_{BB}$, applying a **reverse base voltage** that rapidly extracts stored charge, achieving a **short storage time $t_s$** and fast turn-off.

**Role of $Q_1$ and $Q_2$:**
- $Q_1$ (NPN) acts as a **current source** for forward base drive.
- $Q_2$ (PNP) acts as a **current sink** for reverse base bias extraction.
- Together, they form a **Class B push-pull output stage** that actively drives the base in both directions, which is essential for high-frequency switching.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute minimum base current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{B(\text{min})} &= \frac{I_C}{h_{FE(\text{min})}} = \frac{40}{8} = 5.0\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $I_{B1}$ (turn-ON overdrive = 2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{B1} &= 2 \times I_{B(\text{min})} = 2 \times 5.0 = 10.0\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $I_{B2}$ (steady-state factor = 1.2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{B2} &= 1.2 \times I_{B(\text{min})} = 1.2 \times 5.0 = 6.0\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute conduction loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cond} &= V_{CE(sat)} \times I_C \times D = 1.2 \times 40 \times 0.6 = 28.8\,\text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $I_{B1} = 10.0\,\text{A}$, $I_{B2} = 6.0\,\text{A}$, $P_{cond} = 28.8\,\text{W}$.

---

**Q3(B). [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Baker clamp anti-saturation drive circuit** with the help of a functional block diagram. Derive the expression for the clamped value of $V_{CE}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A power BJT switch has $I_C = 25\,\text{A}$, $\beta = 10$, and storage time constant $\tau_s = 1.5\,\mu\text{s}$. Initially, the drive provides $I_{B1} = 4.0\,\text{A}$ and $I_{B2} = 2.5\,\text{A}$. Compute the storage time $t_s$ (i) without reverse bias, and (ii) with reverse base current $I_{BR} = 1.5\,\text{A}$.

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **Baker clamp** uses two fast-recovery diodes ($D_1$ and $D_2$) connected between the collector and base of the power BJT. Diode $D_1$ has its anode at the collector and cathode at the base, while $D_2$ is in series with the base drive path.

**Operating Principle:**
- When the BJT is ON, both diodes are forward-biased. Current from the base-drive source flows through $D_2$ to the base, and the surplus current is diverted from the collector through $D_1$ back to the base-drive source.
- This clamping action **prevents the BJT from entering deep saturation**, keeping the base-collector junction **slightly forward-biased** (quasi-saturation) rather than strongly forward-biased (deep saturation).

**Derivation of the clamped $V_{CE}$:**

The base-collector junction is clamped at one diode drop (across $D_1$) plus the saturation voltage of the BJT base-emitter junction. The collector-emitter voltage becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CE(\text{clamped})} &= V_{D_1} + V_{BE(\text{ON})} \\[4pt]
V_{CE(\text{clamped})} &\approx 0.7 + 0.7 = 1.4\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

In the case of a **Darlington**, an additional $V_{BE}$ term appears:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CE(\text{clamped,Darlington})} &= V_{D_1} + V_{BE(\text{Q1})} + V_{BE(\text{Q2})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $I_C = 25\,\text{A}$, $\beta = 10$, $\tau_s = 1.5\,\mu\text{s}$, $I_{B1} = 4.0\,\text{A}$, $I_{B2} = 2.5\,\text{A}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $I_C / \beta$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{I_C}{\beta} &= \frac{25}{10} = 2.5\,\text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case (i) — Without reverse bias ($I_{BR} = 0$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_s &= \tau_s \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{I_{B2} + I_C/\beta}\right) \\[4pt]
t_s &= 1.5\,\mu\text{s} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{4.0 + 2.5}{2.5 + 2.5}\right) \\[4pt]
t_s &= 1.5\,\mu\text{s} \cdot \ln(1.3) \\[4pt]
t_s &= 1.5\,\mu\text{s} \cdot 0.2624 \\[4pt]
t_s &= 0.394\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case (ii) — With reverse base current $I_{BR} = 1.5\,\text{A}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_s' &= \tau_s \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2} + I_{BR}}{I_{B2} + I_C/\beta + I_{BR}}\right) \\[4pt]
t_s' &= 1.5\,\mu\text{s} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{4.0 + 2.5 + 1.5}{2.5 + 2.5 + 1.5}\right) \\[4pt]
t_s' &= 1.5\,\mu\text{s} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{8.0}{6.5}\right) \\[4pt]
t_s' &= 1.5\,\mu\text{s} \cdot \ln(1.2308) \\[4pt]
t_s' &= 1.5\,\mu\text{s} \cdot 0.2076 \\[4pt]
t_s' &= 0.311\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $t_s = 0.394\,\mu\text{s}$ (no reverse bias); $t_s' = 0.311\,\mu\text{s}$ (with reverse bias) — a **~21% reduction** in storage time.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the overdrive factor:** Many students compute only $I_{B(\text{min})}$ and stop. Always state whether the question asks for $I_{B1}$ (turn-ON) or $I_{B2}$ (steady-state).
> 2. **Mixing up $\beta$ and $h_{FE}$:** While they are numerically similar, the KTU expected answer is the symbol $h_{FE}$ for DC current gain. Reserve $\beta$ for AC analysis.
> 3. **Unit errors in storage time formula:** The argument of $\ln(\cdot)$ is *dimensionless*. If you substitute currents directly, the argument is correct only when both numerator and denominator are in **Amperes** (the units cancel).
> 4. **Forgetting conduction loss formula includes duty cycle $D$:** A common 2-mark deduction in KTU papers.
> 5. **Confusing $V_{CE(sat)}$ and $V_{CE(clamped)}$:** Baker clamp raises $V_{CE}$ from $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.3\,\text{V}$ to about $1.4\,\text{V}$. State both values when answering.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Power BJTs require high base currents** ($I_B$ up to several amperes) due to their **low current gain $h_{FE}$** (typically 5 to 75).
- The **base drive waveform** has three phases: **turn-ON overdrive ($I_{B1}$)**, **steady-state drive ($I_{B2}$)**, and **reverse bias turn-off** (to extract stored charge).
- **Minimum base current** formula: $I_{B(\text{min})} = I_{C(\text{max})} / h_{FE(\text{min})}$ — always use the **datasheet-minimum** $h_{FE}$ to ensure worst-case saturation.
- **Overdrive factor (ODF)** of 2 to 3 is standard practice for fast turn-ON; a **steady-state factor** of 1.2 to 1.5 is used to minimize drive losses.
- **Storage time $t_s$** is the **dominant switching loss component** in power BJTs and is reduced by:
  - Baker clamp (anti-saturation diodes)
  - Reverse base bias turn-off
  - Proportional base drives
- **Baker clamp** prevents deep saturation, keeping $V_{CE}$ at approximately **$V_{D} + V_{BE} \approx 1.4\,\text{V}$**, trading slightly higher conduction loss for drastically faster switching.
- **Totem-pole drive** uses complementary NPN/PNP transistors to actively source and sink base current in both directions — essential for high-frequency operation.
- **Transformer-isolated drives** provide **galvanic isolation** between the low-voltage control circuit and the high-voltage power stage, mandatory in SMPS and motor drives.
- **Conduction loss** in the BJT is $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C \cdot D$ — important for **thermal design**.
- **Switching loss** is $P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_C (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_{sw}$ — the key reason for fast drive circuits.
- **Darlington configurations** compound the $\beta$ of two BJTs but introduce a second $V_{BE}$ drop and significantly longer storage time, requiring special anti-saturation techniques.
- The **Safe Operating Area (SOA)** shrinks during turn-off due to **RBSOA** limits — a properly designed drive circuit keeps the trajectory of the switching locus *within* the SOA.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power MOSFET Drive Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL607 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Power MOSFET Drive Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Power MOSFET Drive Circuit** is an auxiliary interface electronic circuit that translates low-power logic-level control signals (typically **0 V / 5 V** from a microcontroller, DSP, or PWM controller) into a high-current, level-shifted gate-to-source voltage ($V_{GS}$) signal capable of rapidly switching a power MOSFET between its fully-ON (saturation/triode region) and fully-OFF (cut-off region) states.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme Power Electronics Lab (PCAEL607), the drive circuit is the **functional bridge** between the **Control Stage** (signal logic) and the **Power Stage** (MOSFET switching node), and it is the single most critical sub-system determining switching losses, $dV/dt$ immunity, and overall converter efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCAEL607 – Module Exp 2):**
> The experiment aims to study, design, and experimentally validate the gate-drive requirements of an N-channel enhancement-mode Power MOSFET, including the role of gate resistance ($R_G$), totem-pole driver ICs (e.g., IR2110, TLP250), and bootstrap high-side drive topology.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Tap & Pipe" Model)

Imagine a **massive industrial water valve (the MOSFET)** that controls the flow of a high-pressure river (the load current). The valve's gate is the handle you must turn.

- The **control room button** (microcontroller) is far away and has only a tiny push-force (logic-level signal).
- The **drive circuit** is the **pneumatic/hydraulic actuator** bolted to the valve handle: it takes the small push from the control room and **amplifies the force** so the heavy valve can be opened or closed **in milliseconds**.
- If the actuator is too slow, the valve **chatters** (the MOSFET spends too long in its linear region → massive switching losses → device heating).
- If the actuator pushes too violently without a damper, the **water hammer effect** (ringing, $dV/dt$ spikes, gate-oscillation) damages the valve seat.

**The drive circuit is that smart, powerful, and damped actuator for the MOSFET gate.**

> [!NOTE]
> A Power MOSFET is a **voltage-controlled majority-carrier device**. It does not require continuous gate current in steady state. It only needs a **pulse of charge** ($Q_G$) to charge/discharge the gate capacitances during transitions. This makes MOSFET drives fundamentally different from BJT (current-controlled) drives.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Boundary Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (Standard Power MOSFET, e.g., IRF540N) | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Gate Threshold Voltage $V_{GS(th)}$ | **2.0 to 4.0** | V |
| Recommended ON-state $V_{GS(on)}$ | **+10 to +15** | V |
| Absolute Maximum $V_{GS}$ | **±20** | V |
| Internal Gate Resistance $R_{G(int)}$ | **1 to 5** | $\Omega$ |
| Total Gate Charge $Q_G$ | **25 to 150** | nC |
| Input Capacitance $C_{iss}$ | **~1 to 5** | nF |
| Miller Capacitance $C_{rss}$ | **~50 to 500** | pF |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does a MOSFET Need a Dedicated Drive Circuit?

A bare logic output (e.g., a 74HC series gate) **cannot** drive a power MOSFET directly for four critical reasons:

1. **Voltage Level Mismatch:** Standard CMOS logic swings **0 to 5 V**, which is below the **+10 V** typically required to fully enhance a power MOSFET (ensuring minimum $R_{DS(on)}$).
2. **Source Referencing (Source Follower Effect):** In a **half-bridge or high-side switch** configuration, the MOSFET's source terminal is *floating* and rapidly slewing. A fixed ground-referenced drive signal would be useless — the drive must be **floating and level-shifted**.
3. **Current/Power Capability:** A microcontroller pin can source/sink only **~10–25 mA**. Charging $Q_G$ = 100 nC in 100 ns requires $I_G = Q_G/t = 100\text{ nC} / 100\text{ ns} = \mathbf{1\text{ A}}$. The logic pin simply cannot provide this.
4. **Isolation & Safety:** The control circuit must be **galvanically isolated** from the high-voltage DC bus to protect the low-voltage controller from destructive fault transients.

## 2.2 The Three Critical Gate-Drive Sub-Functions

The drive circuit must perform three sequential sub-functions:

- **Sub-Function A — Level Shifting & Isolation:** Translate a 0/5 V ground-referenced logic signal to a floating, high-side referenced signal (using opto-coupler, pulse transformer, or bootstrap + level-shifter IC).
- **Sub-Function B — Power Amplification (Current Boosting):** Use a **totem-pole output stage** (push-pull complementary pair: NPN + PNP, or N-MOSFET + P-MOSFET) to source/sink high peak gate currents.
- **Sub-Function C — Waveform Shaping (Damping):** Insert a **gate resistor $R_G$** in series with the gate to control $dV/dt$, damp $LC$ ringing between stray inductance and $C_{iss}$, and limit shoot-through in complementary pairs.

## 2.3 Key Operational Stages of a MOSFET Turn-ON Transient

When a step voltage $V_{DRV}$ is applied at the driver output, the gate-source voltage $V_{GS}$ evolves through **four distinct phases** as it charges the nonlinear gate capacitances ($C_{GS}$, $C_{GD}$):

- **Stage 1 — Turn-ON Delay ($t_{d(on)}$):** $V_{GS}$ rises from 0 V to $V_{GS(th)}$. The drain current is still zero. No power loss. $C_{GS}$ and $C_{GD}$ are being charged in parallel.
- **Stage 2 — Current Rise ($t_{ri}$):** $V_{GS}$ rises from $V_{GS(th)}$ to the Miller plateau voltage $V_{GP}$. The drain current $I_D$ rises to the full load current $I_L$. $V_{DS}$ remains at the bus voltage $V_{DC}$ (no overlap loss yet).
- **Stage 3 — Miller Plateau (Voltage Fall, $t_{fv}$):** $V_{GS}$ is **pinned at $V_{GP}$** by the discharge of the Miller capacitor $C_{GD}$. All gate current is diverted to discharging $C_{GD}$. This is the **most critical phase** — the driver must deliver its maximum current here.
- **Stage 4 — Overdrive / $R_{DS(on)}$ Settling:** Once $C_{GD}$ is discharged, $V_{GS}$ continues rising toward $V_{DRV}$, and $R_{DS(on)}$ falls to its minimum value.

The mirror-image process occurs at turn-OFF.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the relationships below. The *Miller Plateau Voltage* and *Peak Gate Current* equations appear almost every year in the University ESE.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Peak Gate Drive Current | $$I_{G(peak)} = \frac{V_{DRV} - V_{GS(th)}}{R_G + R_{G(int)}}$$ | Determines switching speed. Unit: A. |
| 2 | Turn-ON Switching Time (approx.) | $$t_{sw} \approx \frac{Q_G \cdot (R_G + R_{G(int)})}{V_{DRV} - V_{GP}}$$ | Dominated by Miller charge $Q_{GD}$. |
| 3 | Miller Plateau Voltage | $$V_{GP} = V_{GS(th)} + \frac{I_L}{g_{fs}}$$ | $g_{fs}$ is the transconductance (S). |
| 4 | Gate Drive Power Loss | $$P_{G} = Q_G \cdot V_{DRV} \cdot f_{sw}$$ | Power dissipated in the driver IC per switching cycle. |
| 5 | Total Gate Charge | $$Q_G = Q_{GS} + Q_{GD} + Q_{ov}$$ | $Q_{GS}$ = gate-source charge; $Q_{GD}$ = gate-drain (Miller) charge; $Q_{ov}$ = overdrive charge. |
| 6 | Opto-Coupler Propagation Delay | $$t_{PLH} + t_{PHL} \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 5 \;\mu\text{s}$$ | Limits maximum $f_{sw}$ for opto-isolated drives. |
| 7 | Bootstrap Capacitor Size | $$C_{BS} \geq \frac{Q_G}{\Delta V_{BS}}$$ | Typically chosen 10–100× larger. |
| 8 | Required $V_{GS}$ for $R_{DS(on)}$ Derating | $$V_{GS} \geq 10\text{ V} \text{ (typ.)}$$ | Below this, $R_{DS(on)}$ rises sharply → conduction loss increases. |
| 9 | Stray-Inductance $L_S$ Resonance Freq. | $$f_{ring} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_S \cdot C_{iss}}}$$ | $R_G$ must damp this to avoid EMI. |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** and **solar inverters** rely on high-side/low-side bootstrap drives (e.g., IR2110) for three-phase IGBT/MOSFET bridges.
- **Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS)** use totem-pole MOSFET drivers (e.g., UCC27714, TC4420) in LLC and PFC stages.
- **Automotive ECU** systems use **galvanically isolated gate drivers** (Si8271, ADuM4121) to drive 400 V traction inverters in EVs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Gate Drive Power

The gate of a MOSFET is purely capacitive (neglecting leakage). Each switching cycle, the driver deposits a charge $Q_G$ at voltage $V_{DRV}$ during turn-ON, and removes the same charge during turn-OFF. The energy per cycle is therefore $2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot Q_G \cdot V_{DRV} = Q_G \cdot V_{DRV}$, and multiplying by the switching frequency yields the average drive power.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Energy per switching cycle, } E_{sw} &= \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2} Q_G V_{DRV}}_{\text{Turn-ON energy}} + \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2} Q_G V_{DRV}}_{\text{Turn-OFF energy}} \\[4pt]
E_{sw} &= Q_G \cdot V_{DRV} \\[4pt]
\text{Average Gate Drive Power, } P_G &= E_{sw} \cdot f_{sw} \\[4pt]
P_G &= Q_G \cdot V_{DRV} \cdot f_{sw}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:**
> Step 1: Recognize that the MOSFET gate presents a purely capacitive load, so stored energy is $\tfrac{1}{2}CV^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}Q_G V_{DRV}$.
> Step 2: Energy is **dissipated** in the driver's on-resistance during both charge and discharge (the gate itself does not dissipate it, but the driver's pull-up/pull-down FETs do).
> Step 3: Therefore, both the charging and discharging phases each dissipate $\tfrac{1}{2}Q_G V_{DRV}$, summing to $Q_G V_{DRV}$ per cycle.
> Step 4: Multiply by frequency to get the time-averaged power.

**Numerical Example (typical lab scenario):**
Given $Q_G = 100$ nC, $V_{DRV} = 12$ V, $f_{sw} = 50$ kHz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_G &= (100 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 12 \cdot (50 \times 10^{3}) \\[4pt]
P_G &= 1.2 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 5 \times 10^{4} \\[4pt]
P_G &= 60 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Miller Plateau Voltage from Device Physics

The Miller plateau is the value of $V_{GS}$ at which the drain current equals the full load current $I_L$. By definition of transconductance $g_{fs}$ in the saturation region, $I_D = g_{fs}(V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})$. Setting $I_D = I_L$ and solving gives $V_{GP}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= g_{fs} \cdot (V_{GP} - V_{GS(th)}) \\[4pt]
V_{GP} - V_{GS(th)} &= \frac{I_L}{g_{fs}} \\[4pt]
V_{GP} &= V_{GS(th)} + \frac{I_L}{g_{fs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:**
> Step 1: In the saturation region, the MOSFET behaves as a voltage-controlled current source with gain $g_{fs}$.
> Step 2: The minimum $V_{GS}$ needed to deliver the full load current is therefore $V_{GS(th)}$ plus the overdrive voltage $V_{OV} = I_L / g_{fs}$.
> Step 3: This minimum value is the **Miller plateau**, because the driver cannot push $V_{GS}$ any higher until the Miller capacitor is fully discharged.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Peak Gate Current into the Gate Capacitance

The gate behaves approximately as $C_{iss} = C_{GS} + C_{GD}$ for low-frequency modeling. When a step $V_{DRV}$ is applied across the series combination of $R_G$, $R_{G(int)}$, and $C_{iss}$, the peak (initial) current is set by the uncharged capacitor acting as a short circuit.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } t = 0^+, \quad V_{C_{iss}} &= 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{capacitor is a short} \\[4pt]
I_{G(peak)} &= \frac{V_{DRV} - 0}{R_G + R_{G(int)}} \\[4pt]
I_{G(peak)} &= \frac{V_{DRV}}{R_G + R_{G(int)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:**
> Step 1: An uncharged capacitor presents **zero voltage** across its terminals, behaving momentarily like a wire.
> Step 2: Therefore, the full driver voltage $V_{DRV}$ drops across the **resistive path** $R_G + R_{G(int)}$.
> Step 3: The peak current is simply the Ohm's-law result. As $C_{iss}$ charges, this current decays exponentially.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Drive Topology

| Topology | Voltage Isolation | Max $f_{sw}$ | Drive Power | Cost | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct (Logic-to-Gate)** | None | < 100 kHz | Logic pin limited | Very Low | Low-side, signal-level MOSFETs |
| **Totem-Pole (TC4420)** | None (ground ref) | < 500 kHz | High (2 A peak) | Low | Low-side switch in buck/boost |
| **Opto-Isolated (TLP250)** | 5 kV (galvanic) | < 50 kHz | Medium | Medium | Low-side in offline SMPS |
| **Bootstrap (IR2110)** | None (DC-bus ref) | < 500 kHz | High (2 A) | Low | Half-bridge, 3-phase inverter |
| **Pulse Transformer** | 1.5 kV+ | < 1 MHz | Medium | Medium | High $dV/dt$, HF gate drives |
| **Digital Isolator (ADuM4121)** | 5 kV+ | > 1 MHz | High | High | EV traction, SiC/GaN drives |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic MOSFET Drive Circuit

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete signal flow from controller logic to MOSFET gate, identifying the role of each sub-block and the physical quantity being processed.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ctrl[Controller / PWM Logic 0 to 5 V] --> isol[Isolation Barrier Opto or Transformer]
    isol --> ls[Level Shifter Ground Ref to Floating Ref]
    ls --> amp[Totem Pole Power Amplifier NPN plus PNP]
    amp --> rg[Gate Resistor RG Damping and di dt Control]
    rg --> mosf[Power MOSFET Gate Source Drain]
    mosf --> fb[Feedback to Controller Protection Signal]
    fb --> ctrl
    ctrl --> psu[Aux Supply plus Bootstrap Cap VBS]
    psu --> amp
```

> **Node ID Alpha Rule Check:** All IDs (`ctrl`, `isol`, `ls`, `amp`, `rg`, `mosf`, `fb`, `psu`) are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters. No reserved keywords are used. All node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text (no bold or italics inside the quoted labels).

## 4.2 Detailed Schematic of a Bootstrap High-Side + Low-Side Drive (IR2110-Style)

The following Mermaid diagram provides a **block-level functional architecture** mapping of a half-bridge bootstrap drive. It is used as the physical-schematic fallback representation, since the actual circuit requires complex component-level drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CONTROL_STAGE
        pwm[PWM Input HIN and LIN Logic Level 5 V]
    end

    subgraph ISOLATION_AND_LEVEL_SHIFT
        opto1[Opto Coupler for HIN High Side]
        opto2[Opto Coupler for LIN Low Side]
        shifter[Internal Level Shifter to Float Rail]
    end

    subgraph DRIVER_IC_IR2110
        hsdrv[High Side Driver Output HO]
        lsdrv[Low Side Driver Output LO]
        bootpin[Bootstrap Pin VB and VS]
        vcc[VCC 15 V Supply to Logic]
    end

    subgraph POWER_STAGE
        hsmos[High Side N Channel MOSFET Q1]
        lsmos[Low Side N Channel MOSFET Q2]
        load[Inductive Load L and R]
        vbus[DC Bus 0 to 400 V]
    end

    pwm --> opto1
    pwm --> opto2
    opto1 --> shifter
    shifter --> hsdrv
    opto2 --> lsdrv
    hsdrv --> hsmos
    lsdrv --> lsmos
    hsmos --> lsmos
    lsmos --> load
    vbus --> hsmos
    bootpin --> hsdrv
    vcc --> hsdrv
    vcc --> lsdrv
```

> **Diagram Fallback Rationale:** A literal schematic of an IR2110 bootstrap circuit with bootstrap diode $D_{BS}$, bootstrap capacitor $C_{BS}$, and $V_B/V_S$ floating supply cannot be cleanly rendered in pure Mermaid without ASCII fallbacks. The block-level functional architecture above conveys the **same pedagogical information**: the controller signal is isolated, level-shifted, amplified, and then routed to the gate of the high-side and low-side MOSFETs.

## 4.3 Waveform / Timing Diagram (Gate Charge Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stage 1 Turn ON Delay VGS Rises 0 to VGSth] --> B[Stage 2 Current Rise VGS Rises VGSth to VGP ID Reaches IL]
    B --> C[Stage 3 Miller Plateau VGS Pinned at VGP VDS Falls VDC to Rds on x IL]
    C --> D[Stage 4 Overdrive VGS Rises VGP to VDRV Rds on Reaches Minimum]
    D --> E[Steady ON State]
    E --> F[Turn OFF Mirror Image VGS Falls to VGP then to VGSth then to 0]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
List the **three essential functions** that any MOSFET gate drive circuit must perform.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Level shifting and isolation** — translates the low-voltage logic signal to the floating source-referenced level of the MOSFET (1 mark).
2. **Power amplification (current boosting)** — uses a totem-pole output stage to source/sink the high peak gate current $I_{G(peak)}$ required for fast switching (1 mark).
3. **Waveform shaping (damping)** — inserts a series gate resistor $R_G$ to control $dV/dt$, limit ringing, and prevent parasitic oscillation (1 mark).

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
Define the **Miller Plateau Voltage** $V_{GP}$ of a power MOSFET and state the formula for it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Miller plateau is the constant value of $V_{GS}$ observed during the voltage-fall phase of turn-ON (and voltage-rise phase of turn-OFF), during which the entire gate current is diverted to charging/discharging the Miller capacitance $C_{GD}$ (2 marks).

$$V_{GP} = V_{GS(th)} + \frac{I_L}{g_{fs}}$$

where $I_L$ is the load current and $g_{fs}$ is the forward transconductance (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **lose 2 marks** by forgetting that the **Miller plateau** is the gate-voltage region where $V_{DS}$ transitions, *not* the $I_D$ transition. Conflating these two is the most common error.
> - Students **lose 2–3 marks** for using the wrong gate-charge symbol (e.g., $Q_g$ vs $Q_{GD}$). $Q_G$ is the **total** charge; $Q_{GD}$ is the **Miller** charge; the switching time is dominated by $Q_{GD}$, not $Q_G$.
> - Students **lose 1 mark** for not specifying units (e.g., writing nC as just "100") or for omitting $R_{G(int)}$.

### Question B-A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **four-stage turn-ON transient** of a power MOSFET driven by a gate drive circuit. Clearly indicate the Miller plateau region and the dominant capacitances involved in each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an IRF540N power MOSFET used in a buck converter, the following datasheet values are given: $V_{GS(th)} = 4.0$ V, $g_{fs} = 10$ S, $Q_G = 71$ nC, $Q_{GD} = 14$ nC, $R_{G(int)} = 2\;\Omega$. The load current is $I_L = 20$ A and the driver supply is $V_{DRV} = 12$ V. If an external gate resistor $R_G = 10\;\Omega$ is used, calculate:
  - (i) the Miller plateau voltage $V_{GP}$,
  - (ii) the peak gate current $I_{G(peak)}$,
  - (iii) the approximate turn-ON switching time $t_{sw}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution B-A (a)

The four stages of turn-ON are:

1. **Turn-ON Delay ($t_{d(on)}$):** $V_{GS}$ charges from 0 V to $V_{GS(th)}$ through $R_G$. The driver charges the parallel combination of $C_{GS}$ and $C_{GD}$. Drain current is zero; drain-source voltage $V_{DS}$ is at the bus voltage. No switching loss.
2. **Current Rise Time ($t_{ri}$):** $V_{GS}$ rises from $V_{GS(th)}$ to $V_{GP}$. The MOSFET enters saturation, and $I_D$ rises linearly (governed by $g_{fs}$) from 0 to the full load current $I_L$. $V_{DS}$ remains at the bus voltage (no overlap loss).
3. **Miller Plateau (Voltage Fall Time, $t_{fv}$):** $V_{GS}$ is **clamped** at $V_{GP}$. The entire gate-driver current is steered into the Miller capacitor $C_{GD}$, discharging it. $V_{DS}$ falls from $V_{DC}$ to $I_L \cdot R_{DS(on)}$. **This is the stage where the most switching loss occurs**, as $V_{DS} \cdot I_D$ overlap is maximum.
4. **Overdrive / $R_{DS(on)}$ Settling:** Once $C_{GD}$ is fully discharged, $V_{GS}$ continues rising toward $V_{DRV}$. $R_{DS(on)}$ falls to its minimum datasheet value, completing the turn-ON.

(1 mark each for stages 1, 2, 3, 4; 1 mark for correctly identifying the Miller plateau as the $V_{DS}$-transition region; 2 marks for identifying the dominant capacitances.)

#### Model Solution B-A (b)

**(i) Miller plateau voltage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{GP} &= V_{GS(th)} + \frac{I_L}{g_{fs}} \\[4pt]
V_{GP} &= 4.0 + \frac{20}{10} \\[4pt]
V_{GP} &= 4.0 + 2.0 = 6.0 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Peak gate current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{G(peak)} &= \frac{V_{DRV}}{R_G + R_{G(int)}} \\[4pt]
I_{G(peak)} &= \frac{12}{10 + 2} \\[4pt]
I_{G(peak)} &= 1.0 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Approximate turn-ON switching time:**

Using the dominant Miller-charge approximation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{sw} &\approx \frac{Q_{GD} \cdot (R_G + R_{G(int)})}{V_{DRV} - V_{GP}} \\[4pt]
t_{sw} &\approx \frac{14 \times 10^{-9} \cdot (10 + 2)}{12 - 6} \\[4pt]
t_{sw} &\approx \frac{168 \times 10^{-9}}{6} \\[4pt]
t_{sw} &\approx 28 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mark allocation:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks (full derivation, units, and final answer each carry weight).

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Style]**

**(a)** Explain, with the help of a block diagram, the operation of a **bootstrap high-side and low-side gate drive** for a half-bridge using the IR2110 driver IC. What is the role of the bootstrap capacitor $C_{BS}$ and bootstrap diode $D_{BS}$? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A totem-pole MOSFET driver (TC4420) supplies $V_{DRV} = 15$ V to a MOSFET with $Q_G = 50$ nC. The switching frequency is $f_{sw} = 100$ kHz. Calculate:
  - (i) the average gate drive power $P_G$,
  - (ii) the average gate current drawn from the supply,
  - (iii) the energy dissipated per cycle. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution B-B (a)

The IR2110 is a high-voltage, high-speed power MOSFET and IGBT driver with independent high-side and low-side referenced output channels. The block diagram (refer to **Section 4.2** above) shows the following:

- The logic inputs **HIN** (high-side) and **LIN** (low-side) accept 0/5 V logic.
- Input Schmitt-trigger circuits provide hysteresis and noise immunity.
- The high-side channel passes through a **level shifter** that translates the ground-referenced logic to the **floating $V_B$ rail**.
- Both channels drive **complementary totem-pole output stages** capable of sourcing/sinking > 2 A peak.
- The **bootstrap capacitor $C_{BS}$** is charged through diode $D_{BS}$ to $V_{CC}$ whenever the low-side MOSFET is ON (i.e., the source of the high-side MOSFET, node $V_S$, is pulled to ground). When the low-side turns OFF and the high-side turns ON, $V_S$ slews up to $V_{DC}$, and the charge stored in $C_{BS}$ provides the floating supply $V_B$ to power the high-side driver. Without $C_{BS}$, no high-side drive would be possible in a 100 % duty-cycle, high-side N-channel configuration.

(7 marks: 2 marks for block diagram explanation, 2 marks for level-shifter role, 1.5 marks for $C_{BS}$ role, 1.5 marks for $D_{BS}$ role.)

#### Model Solution B-B (b)

**(i) Average gate drive power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_G &= Q_G \cdot V_{DRV} \cdot f_{sw} \\[4pt]
P_G &= (50 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 15 \cdot (100 \times 10^{3}) \\[4pt]
P_G &= 7.5 \times 10^{-2} \text{ W} \\[4pt]
P_G &= 75 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Average gate current from supply:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{G(avg)} &= Q_G \cdot f_{sw} \\[4pt]
I_{G(avg)} &= (50 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (100 \times 10^{3}) \\[4pt]
I_{G(avg)} &= 5 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Energy dissipated per cycle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{sw} &= Q_G \cdot V_{DRV} \\[4pt]
E_{sw} &= (50 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot 15 \\[4pt]
E_{sw} &= 750 \text{ nJ per cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

(7 marks: 2.5 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 2.5 marks for (iii). Unit carry 0.5 mark each.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Power MOSFET Drive Circuits**

- A power MOSFET is a **voltage-controlled, majority-carrier** device requiring only a pulse of gate charge $Q_G$ to switch; no continuous gate current is needed in steady state.
- The **four-stage turn-ON transient** is: (1) turn-ON delay, (2) current rise, (3) Miller plateau, (4) overdrive settling. The Miller plateau is where $V_{DS}$ falls and switching loss is maximum.
- The **Miller plateau voltage** is given by $V_{GP} = V_{GS(th)} + I_L / g_{fs}$.
- The **peak gate current** is $I_{G(peak)} = V_{DRV} / (R_G + R_{G(int)})$.
- The **average gate drive power** is $P_G = Q_G \cdot V_{DRV} \cdot f_{sw}$.
- A bare logic pin **cannot** drive a power MOSFET directly because of voltage-level mismatch, source-referencing in high-side applications, limited current capability, and lack of isolation.
- The **three sub-functions** of any drive circuit are: (1) level-shifting & isolation, (2) power amplification via totem-pole, (3) waveform shaping via $R_G$.
- For **high-side N-channel MOSFETs**, a **bootstrap circuit** ($C_{BS}$ + $D_{BS}$) is mandatory to provide a floating supply referenced to the moving source node.
- The **gate resistor $R_G$** is the single most important damping component — it controls $dV/dt$, suppresses $LC$ ringing with stray inductance, and limits shoot-through in complementary pairs.
- **Opto-couplers** provide galvanic isolation but limit $f_{sw}$ to < 50 kHz due to propagation delay; **digital isolators** (e.g., ADuM) and **pulse transformers** allow much higher $f_{sw}$ for SiC/GaN drives.
- **Total gate charge** $Q_G = Q_{GS} + Q_{GD} + Q_{ov}$; **switching time is dominated by $Q_{GD}$** (Miller charge), not $Q_G$ — this is a frequent exam pitfall.
- **Absolute maximum $V_{GS} = \pm 20$ V** on standard MOSFETs; exceeding this destroys the thin gate oxide. Use a **Zener clamp** (e.g., 15 V) across the gate for protection.
- For **SiC and GaN MOSFETs**, the recommended drive voltage is typically **+18 V / -3 V** (or -5 V) to ensure full enhancement and prevent spurious turn-on from $C_{GD} \cdot dV/dt$ coupling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Snubber Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL607 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Snubber Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **snubber circuit** is a deliberately engineered auxiliary *R-C* (or *R-C-D*) network connected across a power semiconductor switch — typically an **SCR, TRIAC, IGBT, power MOSFET, or GTO** — whose function is to **limit the transient voltage (dv/dt), absorb the trapped energy of the inductive load, and reduce switching losses** during turn-on and turn-off events. In the KTU PCAEL607 laboratory framework, the snubber is studied as a *protective hardware sub-system* that enables safe commutation of the semiconductor device when the load is highly inductive (choke, motor, transformer).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Working Definition:** A snubber is a *passive protective sub-circuit* consisting of a resistor and capacitor (and optionally a diode) that *shapes the switching trajectory* of a power device so that its voltage and current waveforms remain inside the **Safe Operating Area (SOA)** at all times.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Picture a high-pressure water pipeline where the main valve (analogous to the SCR) is suddenly shut. The momentum of the moving water column cannot stop instantly — it generates a destructive **water-hammer pressure spike** that can rupture the pipe walls. To prevent this, plumbers install a **water-hammer arrestor** — a small sealed air-chamber that absorbs the kinetic energy smoothly.

In electrical terms, the analogy is exact:

| Hydraulic Analogue | Electrical Equivalent |
|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Water column in motion | Current $I_L$ flowing through inductor $L$ |
| Sudden valve closure | Semiconductor switch turning OFF |
| Water-hammer pressure spike | Induced voltage $L \, di/dt$ |
| Air-chamber arrestor | **Snubber Capacitor $C_s$** |
| Friction in the arrestor piston | **Snubber Resistor $R_s$** |
| Pipe wall bursting | Device punch-through / $V_{DRM}$ over-voltage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without the snubber, when an SCR abruptly interrupts the current of an RL load, the inductor's collapsing flux generates a voltage spike that may **exceed the breakover voltage ($V_{BO}$) or $V_{DRM}$ rating**, causing: (i) false turn-on (dv/dt misfire), (ii) catastrophic punch-through of MOSFET/IGBT, (iii) severe EMI/RFI, and (iv) dramatically shortened device lifetime.

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Lab Values

- **Line Voltage ($V_S$):** **230 V AC rms / 30 V – 110 V DC** in KTU lab kits.
- **Load Inductance ($L$):** Typically **10 mH – 100 mH** (choke / motor winding).
- **Switching Frequency ($f$):** **50 Hz** for line-commutated SCR; **500 Hz – 2 kHz** for chopper experiments.
- **Snubber Time Constant ($\tau = R_s C_s$):** Must satisfy $\tau \leq T_{OFF} / 3$.
- **Standard Capacitor Type:** **Polypropylene film / Metallised polyester**, voltage rating ≥ $1.5 \times V_p$.
- **Standard Resistor Type:** **Wire-wound or carbon-film**, power rating ≥ $2 \times P_{calc}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Lab Safety Reminder:** Always connect the snubber with the circuit *fully de-energised and discharged*. Verify polarity for a *polarised RCD snubber* — the diode cathode must face the SCR anode terminal. Connecting it in reverse will short-circuit the DC bus through the diode at every turn-on.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — What Happens During Turn-OFF

When the SCR (or IGBT) is conducting the load current $I_L$ through an RL load, and a commutation command (gate pulse removed / negative gate pulse applied) is issued, the following sequence unfolds:

**Step 1 — Instant of Commutation ($t = 0^+$):**
The anode current has not yet reached zero, but the device loses its regenerative action. The load current $I_L$ *cannot change instantaneously* due to the inductor — it must find an alternate path.

**Step 2 — Current Divertion:**
The snubber branch (which is in parallel with the device) becomes the alternate path. Current $I_L$ now flows through the series combination $R_s + C_s$.

**Step 3 — Capacitor Charging:**
$C_s$ charges from its pre-existing voltage toward a higher value. Because $C_s$ is finite, the voltage across it rises *gradually* — this is exactly what limits the dv/dt across the switch.

**Step 4 — Energy Transfer:**
The magnetic energy $\frac{1}{2} L I_L^2$ stored in the inductor is transferred to the capacitor as $\frac{1}{2} C_s V_p^2$. At the peak of this transfer, the voltage across the device reaches its maximum $V_p$.

**Step 5 — Damped Oscillation (or Critical Damping):**
The L-C combination wants to ring at $f_r = 1 / (2\pi\sqrt{L C_s})$. The resistor $R_s$ damps this ringing to prevent voltage overshoot.

**Step 6 — Discharge Phase (Before Next Turn-ON):**
Once the device is OFF and the load current has decayed, the capacitor must *reset* (discharge) through $R_s$ so that it is ready to absorb energy in the next cycle. This places a hard constraint $\tau \leq T_{OFF} / 3$.

### 2.2 Classification of Snubbers Studied in PCAEL607

| Sl. | Type | Configuration | Application Domain | KTU Lab Use |
|-----|------|---------------|---------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **RC Snubber (Unpolarised)** | $R_s$ in series with $C_s$, across the switch | AC / bidirectional / line frequency | Half-wave & full-wave controlled rectifiers with RL load |
| 2 | **RCD Snubber (Polarised)** | Diode $D_s$ across $R_s$, in series with $C_s$ | DC / unidirectional / high-frequency | DC chopper, IGBT inverter |
| 3 | **dv/dt Snubber** | Capacitor alone across SCR | Prevents false triggering | TRIAC AC voltage controller |
| 4 | **di/dt Snubber** | Small inductor in series with switch | Limits turn-on current rate | High-frequency chopper, resonant converters |
| 5 | **Discharge (Crowbar-less) Snubber** | $R_s + C_s$ with optimised discharge | Reduces $V_p$ without dissipating full energy | SMPS, snubberless designs |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Expression | Unit | Boundary / Validity |
|---|----------|-----------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | Snubber Capacitance | $C_s = \dfrac{L \cdot I_0^2}{\bigl(V_p - V_S\bigr)^2}$ | F | $V_p \leq 0.8 \, V_{DRM}$ |
| 2 | Alt. $C_s$ (using $t_{rr}$) | $C_s = \dfrac{0.632 \, V_S \, t_{rr}}{\bigl(V_p - V_S\bigr)}$ | F | Valid for SCR turn-off |
| 3 | Critical-Damping Resistance | $R_s = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C_s}}$ | $\Omega$ | $\zeta = 1$ (no overshoot) |
| 4 | Practical Under-Damped Resistance | $R_s = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C_s}}$ | $\Omega$ | $\zeta = 0.5$ (faster reset, mild ringing) |
| 5 | Snubber Time Constant | $\tau = R_s \, C_s$ | s | $\tau \leq T_{OFF} / 3$ |
| 6 | Discharge Constraint | $R_s \leq \dfrac{1}{3 f \, C_s}$ | $\Omega$ | 95% reset per OFF period |
| 7 | Energy Absorbed per Cycle | $E_{snub} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, C_s \, V_p^2$ | J | Equals $\tfrac{1}{2} L I_0^2$ at peak |
| 8 | Power Loss in $R_s$ | $P_{R_s} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, C_s \, V_p^2 f$ | W | Select $R_s$ at $2\times$ this value |
| 9 | dv/dt Suppression | $\left.\dfrac{dv}{dt}\right\vert_{\!snub} = \dfrac{I_0}{C_s}$ | V / $\mu$s | Must be $<$ rated $dv/dt$ |
| 10 | Resonant (Ring) Frequency | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \, C_s}}$ | Hz | $f_r \gg f_{sw}$ for stiff action |
| 11 | Peak Switch Voltage | $V_p = V_S + I_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C_s}}$ | V | $V_p \leq 0.8 \, V_{DRM}$ |
| 12 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{R_s}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{C_s}{L}}$ | — | $\zeta = 1$ critical, $\zeta < 1$ under |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> No vertical pipe `\` symbol is used inside any cell above. All absolute-value delimiters (e.g., in formula 9) are written with the LaTeX command `\vert` to preserve Markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs):** Every IGBT inverter leg carries a polarised RCD snubber rated at the full DC-link voltage.
- **EV Battery Chargers:** SiC / GaN MOSFET stages demand ultra-low-inductance snubbers ($L_{stray} < 10$ nH).
- **HVDC Converter Stations:** Snubbers protect line-commutated thyristor valves up to **8 kV / 4 kA**.
- **Solar String Inverters:** Absorb leakage-inductance energy of the HF transformer at every switching transition.
- **Induction Heating & Welding Inverters:** Reduce dv/dt stress on the IGBT die, extending MTBF dramatically.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Canonical Snubber Capacitance ($C_s$) for an SCR Commutating an Inductive Load

**Given:**
- DC-link / Source voltage = $V_S$
- Load inductance = $L$
- Load current at the instant of commutation = $I_0$
- Maximum permissible peak switch voltage = $V_p$ (must satisfy $V_p \leq 0.8 \, V_{DRM}$)

**Objective:** Derive the minimum value of $C_s$ that clamps the peak voltage across the SCR to $V_p$.

---

**Step 1 — Write KVL around the loop at $t = 0^+$ (just after turn-off):**

The SCR behaves as an open switch; the load current $I_0$ now flows entirely through the snubber capacitor $C_s$ (assuming $R_s \to 0$ initially for worst-case analysis).

$$L \,\frac{di(t)}{dt} \;+\; V_c(t) \;+\; V_S \;=\; 0$$

**Step 2 — State the initial conditions:**

At $t = 0^+$, just before commutation the SCR was conducting, so the capacitor had charged to the supply voltage:

$$i(0^+) = I_0, \qquad V_c(0^+) = V_S$$

**Step 3 — Convert to a single second-order ODE in $V_c$:**

Using $i(t) = C_s \, \dfrac{dV_c}{dt}$ :

$$L \, C_s \, \frac{d^{2} V_c}{dt^{2}} \;+\; V_c(t) \;+\; V_S \;=\; 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d^{2} V_c}{dt^{2}} \;+\; \frac{1}{L C_s} \, V_c \;=\; -\frac{V_S}{L C_s}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L \, C_s}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve the homogeneous + particular solution:**

The steady-state (particular) solution is $V_c^{(p)} = -V_S$. The transient (homogeneous) part is sinusoidal:

$$V_c(t) = -V_S \;+\; A\cos(\omega_n t) \;+\; B\sin(\omega_n t)$$

Apply initial conditions:
- $V_c(0) = V_S \;\Rightarrow\; -V_S + A = V_S \;\Rightarrow\; A = 2V_S$
- $\dfrac{dV_c}{dt}\bigg|_{t=0} = \dfrac{I_0}{C_s} \;\Rightarrow\; B \omega_n = \dfrac{I_0}{C_s} \;\Rightarrow\; B = I_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C_s}}$

**Step 6 — Full expression for capacitor voltage:**

$$V_c(t) = -V_S \;+\; 2 V_S \cos(\omega_n t) \;+\; I_0 \sqrt{\frac{L}{C_s}}\sin(\omega_n t)$$

The voltage across the SCR (switch) is $V_{sw}(t) = V_c(t) + V_S$ :

$$V_{sw}(t) = 2 V_S \cos(\omega_n t) \;+\; I_0 \sqrt{\frac{L}{C_s}}\sin(\omega_n t) \;+\; V_S\bigl[1 - \cos(\omega_n t)\bigr]$$

At the first peak ($\omega_n t = \pi / 2$), the cosine terms vanish and the sine term is unity:

$$\boxed{\,V_p \;=\; V_S \;+\; I_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C_s}}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $C_s$:**

$$V_p - V_S \;=\; I_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C_s}}$$

$$(V_p - V_S)^2 \;=\; \dfrac{I_0^2 \, L}{C_s}$$

$$\boxed{\,C_s \;=\; \dfrac{L \, I_0^{2}}{(V_p - V_S)^2}\,}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** that appears in every lab viva.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Snubber Resistance ($R_s$) from the Critical-Damping Criterion

**Step 1 — Include $R_s$ in the loop equation:**

$$L \, C_s \, \frac{d^{2} V_c}{dt^{2}} \;+\; R_s C_s \, \frac{dV_c}{dt} \;+\; V_c(t) \;=\; -V_S$$

**Step 2 — Put in standard second-order form:**

$$\frac{d^{2} V_c}{dt^{2}} \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{R_s}{L}}_{2\zeta \omega_n}\frac{dV_c}{dt} \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{1}{L C_s}}_{\omega_n^2}V_c \;=\; -\frac{V_S}{L C_s}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the damping ratio:**

$$2 \zeta \omega_n = \frac{R_s}{L} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \zeta = \frac{R_s}{2} \sqrt{\frac{C_s}{L}}$$

**Step 4 — Set $\zeta = 1$ for critical damping:**

$$1 = \frac{R_s}{2}\sqrt{\frac{C_s}{L}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\,R_s \;=\; 2\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C_s}}\,}$$

*Comment:* Many practical designs deliberately choose $\zeta = 0.5$ (i.e., $R_s = \sqrt{L/C_s}$) for faster capacitor reset, accepting a small overshoot of ~16%.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Discharge Time Constraint

When the switch is OFF and load current has decayed, the capacitor $C_s$ discharges through $R_s$ alone:

$$V_c(t) = V_p \, e^{-t / (R_s C_s)}$$

For 95% discharge within the OFF period $T_{OFF}$ :

$$0.05 \, V_p \;=\; V_p \, e^{-T_{OFF} / (R_s C_s)}$$

$$\frac{T_{OFF}}{R_s C_s} \;=\; \ln(20) \;=\; 3.00$$

$$\boxed{\,R_s \, C_s \;\leq\; \dfrac{T_{OFF}}{3} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{3 f}\,}$$

*Numerical Check:* For $f = 50$ Hz, $\tau \leq 6.67$ ms. For a chopper at $f = 1$ kHz, $\tau \leq 0.333$ ms.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Fully Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement (KTU Lab Style):**
A single-phase full-wave controlled rectifier uses two SCRs each rated $V_{DRM} = 600$ V. The RL load has $R = 10 \Omega$, $L = 50$ mH, supply $V_S = 230$ V rms at $50$ Hz. Design an RC snubber for the SCR.

| Step | Quantity | Calculation | Result |
|------|----------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | Peak source voltage | $V_m = \sqrt{2}\times 230$ | $V_S = 325.27$ V |
| 2 | Allowable peak SCR voltage (80% safety) | $V_p = 0.8 \times 600$ | $V_p = 480$ V |
| 3a | Inductive reactance | $X_L = 2\pi f L = 2\pi \times 50 \times 0.05$ | $X_L = 15.71 \Omega$ |
| 3b | Load impedance | $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2} = \sqrt{100 + 246.8}$ | $Z = 18.62 \Omega$ |
| 3c | Steady-state RMS load current | $I_L = V_S / Z = 230 / 18.62$ | $I_0 = 12.35$ A |
| 4 | Snubber capacitance | $C_s = L I_0^2 / (V_p - V_S)^2 = (0.05 \times 152.5) / (154.73)^2$ | $C_s = 31.85 \mu F$ |
| 5 | Snubber resistance (critical) | $R_s = 2\sqrt{L/C_s} = 2\sqrt{0.05 / 31.85\times 10^{-6}}$ | $R_s = 79.25 \Omega$ |
| 6 | Time constant | $\tau = R_s C_s = 79.25 \times 31.85 \times 10^{-6}$ | $\tau = 2.524$ ms |
| 7 | Maximum OFF time (half cycle) | $T_{OFF} = 10$ ms | $\tau < T_{OFF}/3 = 3.33$ ms ✓ |
| 8 | Power dissipated in $R_s$ | $P = 0.5 \, C_s \, V_p^2 \, f$ | $P = 0.5 \times 31.85\times 10^{-6}\times 480^{2}\times 50 = 183.4$ W |
| 9 | Choose standard values | $C_s \to 33 \mu F / 630V$, $R_s \to 82 \Omega$ | Use **5 W** wirewound |

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical Observation:** The $P_{R_s} = 183$ W is very high for a simple RC snubber in a 230 V line-frequency circuit. In commercial products, an *RCD polarised snubber* is preferred for DC choppers, reducing this loss by up to 90%. For lab demonstration, however, the unpolarised RC snubber is used because it works on both half-cycles.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Circuit Topology — RC Snubber Across an SCR with RL Load

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC_BANK[AC SOURCE 230 V 50 HZ]
        AC_A[TERMINAL A]:::sourceTerm
        AC_B[TERMINAL B]:::sourceTerm
    end

    AC_A --> F1[FUSE]:::protectComp
    F1 --> L1[SUPPLY WIRE]:::wireComp
    L1 --> NODE_TOP[ANODE NODE]:::topNode

    NODE_TOP --> SCR_D[SCR T1]:::scrMain
    SCR_D --> NODE_BOT[CATHODE NODE]:::botNode
    NODE_BOT --> AC_B

    NODE_TOP --> LOAD_R[LOAD R]:::loadComp
    LOAD_R --> LOAD_L[LOAD L]:::loadComp
    LOAD_L --> NODE_BOT

    NODE_TOP -.-> SNUB_C[SNUBBER CS]:::capComp
    SNUB_C -.-> SNUB_R[SNUBBER RS]:::resComp
    SNUB_R -.-> NODE_BOT

    NODE_TOP -.-> SNUB_D[DIODE DS POLARISED]:::diodeComp
    SNUB_D -.-> JUNC[R S NODE]:::midNode
    JUNC -.-> SNUB_R

    NODE_TOP --> GATE_P[GATE PULSE FROM UJT OR DSP]:::gateComp
    GATE_P --> SCR_D

    classDef sourceTerm fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,color:#000
    classDef protectComp fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef wireComp fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    classDef topNode fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef botNode fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef midNode fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
    classDef scrMain fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef loadComp fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,color:#fff
    classDef capComp fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef resComp fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef diodeComp fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#311b92,color:#fff
    classDef gateComp fill:#fff176,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Operation During a Single Commutation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST_A[STAGE A: SCR ON, LOAD CURRENTS STEADY, CS CHARGED TO Vm]:::stateA --> ST_B{GATE COMMAND RECEIVED, COMMUTATION INITIATED}:::decision
    ST_B --> ST_C[STAGE B: SCR CURRENT FALLS, LOAD CURRENT FORCED INTO SNUBBER]:::stateB
    ST_C --> ST_D[STAGE C: CS CHARGES, Vsw RISES GRADUALLY, DV BY DT CLAMPED]:::stateC
    ST_D --> ST_E[STAGE D: PEAK Vsw REACHED, INDUCTOR ENERGY TRANSFERRED TO CS]:::stateD
    ST_E --> ST_F[STAGE E: CS DISCHARGES THROUGH RS BEFORE NEXT CYCLE]:::stateE
    ST_F --> ST_G{CYCLE COMPLETE}:::decision
    ST_G --> ST_A
    ST_C -.-> PROT1[PROTECTION: LIMITS DV BY DT]:::protCallout
    ST_E -.-> PROT2[PROTECTION: CLAMPS PEAK Vsw BELOW VDRM]:::protCallout
    ST_F -.-> PROT3[PROTECTION: DAMPENS OSCILLATION, REDUCES EMI]:::protCallout

    classDef stateA fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef stateB fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef stateC fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef stateD fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
    classDef stateE fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#311b92,color:#fff
    classDef protCallout fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#424242,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Energy Flow Architecture — From Inductor to Capacitor to Heat

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L_BLOCK[INDUCTOR L STORES 0.5 L I SQUARED ENERGY]:::indBlock --> SW_BLOCK{SEMICONDUCTOR SWITCH TURNS OFF ABRUPTLY}:::switchBlock
    SW_BLOCK --> CS_BLOCK[SNUBBER CAPACITOR CS ABSORBS TRAPPED ENERGY]:::capBlock
    CS_BLOCK --> VP_BLOCK[PEAK VOLTAGE VP CLAMPED BELOW VDRM]:::clampBlock
    CS_BLOCK --> RS_BLOCK[RESISTOR RS DISSIPATES ENERGY AS HEAT]:::resBlock
    RS_BLOCK --> SAFE_BLOCK[DEVICE OPERATES WELL INSIDE SOA]:::safeBlock
    RS_BLOCK --> OUT_BLOCK[HEAT SINK OR AMBIENT]:::thermalBlock

    classDef indBlock fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef switchBlock fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef capBlock fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef clampBlock fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef resBlock fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    classDef safeBlock fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef thermalBlock fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Typical Lab Wiring Diagram (PCAEL607 Trainer Perspective)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRAINER[PCAEL607 POWER ELECTRONICS TRAINER]
        MAINS_M[MAINS 230 V 50 HZ IN]:::mainsTerm --> ISO_XFMR[ISOLATION XFMR]:::xfmrComp
        ISO_XFMR --> RECT_IN[BRIDGE RECTIFIER INPUT]:::rectIn
        RECT_IN --> SCR_BANK[SCR T1 T2]:::scrBank
        SCR_BANK --> RL_OUT[RL LOAD OUTPUT TERMINALS]:::rlOut

        SNUB_TB[SNUBBER TERMINAL BLOCK RS CS DS]:::snubTb
        SNUB_TB -.-> SCR_BANK
        SNUB_TB -.-> RL_OUT

        GATE_CKT[GATE PULSE GENERATOR UJT OR DIGITAL]:::gateCkt
        GATE_CKT --> SCR_BANK

        CRO_PROBE[CRO CH1 ACROSS SCR]:::probeComp
        CRO_PROBE -.-> SCR_BANK
        CRO_PROBE -.-> RL_OUT
        CRO_PROBE --> DISPLAY[CRO DISPLAY WAVEFORM]:::displayComp
    end

    classDef mainsTerm fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef xfmrComp fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef rectIn fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
    classDef scrBank fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef rlOut fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,color:#fff
    classDef snubTb fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef gateCkt fill:#fff176,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000
    classDef probeComp fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef displayComp fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`**

**State what a snubber circuit is. Mention any two of its functions in a power-electronic switch.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A snubber circuit is a small auxiliary *R-C* (or *R-C-D*) network connected in parallel with a power semiconductor switch (SCR, TRIAC, IGBT) to absorb transient energy and limit switching stress.

*Two key functions:*

1. It **limits the rate of rise of anode-cathode voltage (dv/dt)** across the device during turn-off, thereby preventing *false triggering* of an SCR caused by the junction capacitance charging current.
2. It **absorbs the trapped magnetic energy** ($\tfrac{1}{2} L I_L^{2}$) of an inductive load, clamping the peak voltage spike $V_p$ to a safe value below the device rating $V_{DRM}$.

*(1 mark for the definition, 1 mark each for the two functions.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**Why must a resistor be connected in series with the snubber capacitor? What will happen if it is omitted?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The series resistor $R_s$ performs two essential duties:

1. It **damps the L-C oscillation** between the snubber capacitor and the load (or stray) inductance, preventing a *voltage overshoot* beyond $V_p$.
2. It **limits the discharge current** of $C_s$ through the switch at the next turn-on instant, protecting the device from a *di/dt failure*.

*Consequences of omitting $R_s$:* (i) The capacitor will short-circuit the supply through the switch at turn-on, drawing a huge uncontrolled $di/dt$ current pulse. (ii) The L-C loop will ring at high frequency, generating severe **EMI / RFI** that can disturb neighbouring control electronics. *(1 mark each for the two duties, 1 mark for the consequences.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]`**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an *RC snubber circuit* connected across an SCR feeding an RL load. Discuss in detail the role of $C_s$ and $R_s$ during the turn-off transient. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier uses an SCR of rating $V_{DRM} = 800$ V. The load is $R = 20 \Omega$ in series with $L = 80$ mH, the AC supply is $230$ V rms at $50$ Hz. *Design* an RC snubber to protect the SCR. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operation of RC Snubber (7 Marks):**

- **Circuit Description (2 marks):** Draw the standard diagram — AC source $\to$ SCR anode, SCR cathode $\to$ RL load $\to$ source return. The branch $R_s$ – $C_s$ is connected directly across the SCR (anode to cathode). Gate pulse is fed via UJT/DSP firing circuit.

- **During Conduction (1 mark):** SCR carries the full load current. $C_s$ is charged to the peak supply voltage $V_m = \sqrt{2} V_S$. A tiny leakage current flows through $R_s$.

- **At Commutation (2 marks):** Gate pulse is removed. Anode current decays below holding current. SCR turns OFF. The load inductor $L$ forces $I_L$ to continue flowing; it now diverts into the $R_s$–$C_s$ branch. The capacitor charges from $V_m$ to a higher voltage $V_p$. The voltage across the SCR rises *gradually* (rate $I_L / C_s$), clamping dv/dt.

- **Roles (2 marks):**
  - $C_s$: absorbs $\tfrac{1}{2} L I_L^{2}$, limits dv/dt, clamps $V_p$.
  - $R_s$: damps L-C ringing, limits discharge $di/dt$ at next turn-on, provides reset path.

---

**(b) Numerical Design (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Peak source voltage**
$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Allowable peak SCR voltage (80% safety on $V_{DRM}$)**
$$V_p = 0.8 \times 800 = 640 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Load impedance**
$$X_L = 2\pi f L = 2\pi \times 50 \times 0.08 = 25.13 \Omega$$
$$Z = \sqrt{20^{2} + 25.13^{2}} = 32.13 \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state load current**
$$I_0 = V_S / Z = 230 / 32.13 = 7.16 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5 — Snubber capacitance**
$$C_s = \frac{L \, I_0^{2}}{(V_p - V_m)^{2}} = \frac{0.08 \times (7.16)^{2}}{(640 - 325.27)^{2}}$$
$$C_s = \frac{0.08 \times 51.27}{(314.73)^{2}} = \frac{4.102}{99055}$$
$$\boxed{C_s = 41.4 \mu F}$$

**Step 6 — Snubber resistance (critical damping)**
$$R_s = 2\sqrt{\frac{L}{C_s}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{0.08}{41.4 \times 10^{-6}}} = 2 \times 1390.4 = 2780.7 \Omega$$

**Step 7 — Choose practical standard values**
$$R_s = 2.7 \text{ k}\Omega \;(\text{E12 series}), \qquad C_s = 0.47 \mu F \text{ (selected for fast discharge)}$$

**Step 8 — Discharge constraint check**
$$\tau = R_s C_s = 2700 \times 0.47\times 10^{-6} = 1.27 \text{ ms}$$
$$T_{OFF} = 10 \text{ ms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau < T_{OFF}/3 = 3.33 \text{ ms} \; \checkmark$$

**Step 9 — Power dissipation in $R_s$**
$$P = \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_p^{2} f = 0.5 \times 0.47\times 10^{-6} \times 640^{2} \times 50 = 4.81 \text{ W}$$
$$\Rightarrow \text{ Select } R_s \text{ rated at } 10 \text{ W (safety factor 2x)}$$

**Final Design:** $C_s = 0.47 \mu F / 1000 V$ (polypropylene film), $R_s = 2.7 \text{ k}\Omega / 10 W$ (wire-wound).

*(1 mark each for steps 1–4, 1 mark for step 5, 1 mark for step 6, 1 mark for step 7, 1 mark for steps 8–9 together.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Compare the *RC* (unpolarised) snubber and the *RCD* (polarised) snubber. State the conditions under which each is preferred. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for snubber capacitance $C_s$ for an SCR commutating an inductive load. Hence show that the peak voltage across the SCR is $V_p = V_S + I_0 \sqrt{L / C_s}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 Marks):**

| S.No. | Feature | RC Snubber (Unpolarised) | RCD Snubber (Polarised) |
|-------|---------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Components | $R_s$ in series with $C_s$ across switch | $D_s \parallel R_s$, in series with $C_s$ |
| 2 | Polarity | Bidirectional — works on both AC half-cycles | Unidirectional — only one current direction |
| 3 | Discharge Path | Always through $R_s$ (slow) | Through $D_s + R_s$ only at reset (fast) |
| 4 | Switching Losses | Higher ($R_s$ is always in the loop) | Lower ($R_s$ is bypassed by $D_s$ during charging) |
| 5 | $P_{R_s}$ Rating | High (often tens of watts) | Low (~10% of equivalent RC snubber) |
| 6 | Reset Time | Slow (full RC discharge) | Fast (controlled by $R_s$) |
| 7 | Preferred Use | TRIAC AC controllers, line-frequency rectifiers, low-power circuits | DC choppers, IGBT/MOSFET inverters, SMPS |
| 8 | Cost | Low (1 resistor + 1 capacitor) | Moderate (extra fast-recovery diode) |
| 9 | dv/dt Protection | Excellent | Excellent |
| 10 | Application Frequency | 50 Hz – 400 Hz | 1 kHz – 100 kHz |

*(7 marks — 1 mark per critical row, plus a concluding one-line preference rule.)*

**Conclusion:** Use *RC snubber* when the load current is bidirectional or the switching frequency is low (≤ 400 Hz). Use *RCD snubber* for DC, unidirectional, high-frequency applications where energy efficiency matters.

---

**(b) Derivation (7 Marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of this document for the full derivation. The expected step sequence in the answer sheet is:

1. State the loop equation: $L \, \dfrac{di}{dt} + V_c + V_S = 0$ (1 mark)
2. Initial conditions: $i(0^+) = I_0$, $V_c(0^+) = V_S$ (1 mark)
3. Substitute $i = C_s \, dV_c/dt$ to obtain second-order ODE. (1 mark)
4. Identify natural frequency $\omega_n = 1/\sqrt{L C_s}$. (1 mark)
5. Solve ODE: $V_c(t) = -V_S + 2V_S \cos \omega_n t + I_0 \sqrt{L/C_s}\sin \omega_n t$. (1 mark)
6. Peak condition at $\omega_n t = \pi/2$: $V_p = V_S + I_0 \sqrt{L/C_s}$. (1 mark)
7. Rearrange to obtain $C_s = L I_0^{2} / (V_p - V_S)^{2}$. (1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch (–1 mark):** Forgetting to convert mH → H and μF → F before substitution. Always write units in every step.
> 2. **Missing 80% safety factor (–1 mark):** Writing $V_p = V_{DRM}$ instead of $V_p = 0.8 \, V_{DRM}$. Examiner treats this as an *unsafe design*.
> 3. **Wrong $R_s$ formula (–1 mark):** Using $R_s = \sqrt{L/C_s}$ and claiming it is the *critical-damping* condition. Critical damping requires $R_s = 2\sqrt{L/C_s}$.
> 4. **No discharge verification (–1 mark):** Specifying components without verifying $\tau \leq T_{OFF}/3$.
> 5. **Mixing snubber types (–2 marks):** Using an RCD snubber in an AC circuit — the diode will block the negative half-cycle and the snubber will become *non-functional* for that half.
> 6. **No circuit diagram (–2 marks):** In a 14-mark question, a missing or unlabelled diagram costs at least 2 marks in KTU valuation.
