> 7. **No current direction for $D_s$ (–1 mark):** In an RCD snubber, forgetting to specify that the *cathode* of $D_s$ faces the SCR anode.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Core Definition:** Snubber = protective *R-C* (or *R-C-D*) network placed in parallel with a power switch.
- **Two primary purposes:** (i) **Limit dv/dt** (prevents false turn-on of SCR), (ii) **Absorb $\tfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}$** energy (clamps peak voltage).
- **Two main types:** *RC* (unpolarised, for AC) and *RCD* (polarised, for DC choppers and IGBT inverters).
- **Canonical capacitance formula:** $C_s = \dfrac{L \, I_0^{2}}{(V_p - V_S)^{2}}$
- **Canonical resistance formulas:** $R_s = 2\sqrt{L/C_s}$ (critical) or $R_s = \sqrt{L/C_s}$ (underdamped, faster reset).
- **Peak switch voltage:** $V_p = V_S + I_0 \sqrt{L/C_s}$
- **Discharge constraint:** $\tau = R_s C_s \leq T_{OFF}/3 = 1/(3f)$.
- **Power loss in resistor:** $P_{R_s} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_p^{2} f$ — derate the resistor to **2×** this value.
- **Design safety rule:** Always choose $V_p \leq 0.8 \, V_{DRM}$.
- **RCD polarity rule:** Cathode of $D_s$ at the *anode terminal* of the switch; anode of $D_s$ at the $R_s$–$C_s$ junction.
- **Lab waveform signature:** With the snubber connected, the SCR anode-cathode voltage waveform shows a *rounded, exponentially rising edge* instead of a sharp vertical spike — this is the visual proof that the snubber is functional.
- **Typical lab values:** $R_s \in [10 \Omega, 1 \text{ k}\Omega]$, $C_s \in [0.01 \mu F, 1 \mu F]$ (polypropylene film), $V_{cap\,rating} \geq 1.5 \times V_p$.
- **Favourite viva question:** *"What is the difference between a snubber and a free-wheeling diode?"* — A free-wheeling diode provides a *current path during conduction*, while a snubber *absorbs voltage transients during commutation*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three Phase Diode Bridge Rectifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAEL607 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Three Phase Diode Bridge Rectifier # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Three-Phase Diode Bridge Rectifier (6-Pulse Rectifier)** > A power electronic converter circuit that uses **six uncontrolled power diodes** arranged in a **Graetz bridge** configuration to convert a balanced three-phase AC supply (either directly or through a three-phase transformer) into a relatively smooth DC output. Because six diodes commutate every 60° of the AC cycle, the output exhibits **six pulses per input cycle**, earning it the designation **"6-pulse rectifier."** The circuit naturally produces a ripple frequency of **f_r = 6 × f_s**, where f_s is the supply frequency (50 Hz in India → **f_r = 300 Hz**). ### Circuit Topology Snapshot The bridge consists of two diode groups, each containing three diodes: - **Positive (Upper) Group:** D1, D3, D5 — connected to the three phases and feeding the **positive DC bus (+V_dc)**. - **Negative (Lower) Group:** D2, D4, D6 — connected to the three phases and tied to the **negative DC bus (–V_dc)**. Each phase therefore has one diode pointing toward the positive rail and one diode pointing toward the negative rail, yielding the canonical full-bridge layout. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Six-Valve Water Manifold" Imagine **three pressurized water pipes** (R, Y, B) feeding a common header. Each pipe has **two non-return (check) valves** — one allowing water out to a **top reservoir** and another allowing return from a **bottom reservoir**. Because water cannot flow backwards: 1. The pipe with the **highest instantaneous pressure** will be the one sending water to the top reservoir (its "upper" valve opens). 2. The pipe with the **lowest instantaneous pressure** will be the one accepting return water from the bottom reservoir (its "lower" valve opens). 3. All other valves remain shut. Over one full rotation of the 3-phase pressure cycle, the "highest" and "lowest" pipes change six times — exactly mimicking the **six conduction intervals** of the diode bridge. The reservoir fills almost continuously with very little ripple because the highest-pressure pipe swaps to the next one every **60°** rather than **180°** as in a single-phase bridge. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Takeaway for the Lab:** With a 50 Hz mains, your output ripple will be **300 Hz**, and the no-load average DC voltage will be roughly **1.35 × V_LL** (RMS line-to-line), i.e., about **2.34 × V_phase** (RMS phase). ## 1.3 Why Three-Phase Instead of Single-Phase? | Feature | Single-Phase Full Bridge | Three-Phase Full Bridge | |---|---|---| | Diodes | 4 | 6 | | Output pulses/cycle | 2 | **6** | | Ripple frequency (50 Hz mains) | 100 Hz | **300 Hz** | | Typical DC voltage (same V_LL) | 0.9 V_LL | **1.35 V_LL** | | Typical ripple factor (R load) | ~48 % | **~4.2 %** | | Transformer utilization | Poor | Excellent | > [!TIP] > For high-power industrial applications (DC motor drives, HVDC, electrolysis, battery chargers, UPS front-ends), the **3-phase bridge is the universal first-stage AC-DC converter** because of its inherently low ripple and high DC utilization of the AC supply. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — The 60° Conduction Story For a balanced, purely resistive (or highly inductive) load, the conduction logic is: - **Upper group:** The diode whose anode is at the **highest instantaneous phase potential** conducts. This "winners-take-all" rule is enforced by the unidirectional nature of diodes. - **Lower group:** The diode whose cathode is at the **lowest instantaneous phase potential** conducts. - **Result:** Exactly **two diodes** conduct at any instant — one upper, one lower — and the output voltage equals the **difference between the highest and lowest phase voltages**, i.e., the largest available line-to-line voltage. ### Conduction Sequence (R-Y-B Phase Order, R positive-going first) | Interval (ωt) | Highest Phase | Lowest Phase | Upper Diode | Lower Diode | Output = | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 30° – 90° | R | Y | D1 | D4 | V_RY | | 90° – 150° | R | B | D1 | D6 | V_RB | | 150° – 210° | Y | B | D3 | D6 | V_YB | | 210° – 270° | Y | R | D3 | D2 | V_YR | | 270° – 330° | B | R | D5 | D2 | V_BR | | 330° – 360°/0°–30° | B | Y | D5 | D4 | V_BY | > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice that **D1 and D4 each conduct for 120°**, **D3 and D6 each conduct for 120°**, and **D5 and D2 each conduct for 120°** — the conduction pattern is fully symmetric. Each diode therefore carries current for one-third of the cycle. ## 2.2 Output Voltage Waveform The output is the **positive envelope of the three line-to-line voltages**, each of which is sinusoidal with peak amplitude **V_mL = √3 × V_m**, phase-shifted by 30° from its respective phase voltage. The repeating segment over 60° (from 30° to 90°) is: $$V_{out}(\omega t) = V_{mL} \sin\!\left(\omega t + \frac{\pi}{6}\right), \qquad \frac{\pi}{6} \le \omega t \le \frac{\pi}{2}$$ This pattern repeats **six times per fundamental cycle**, producing a six-humped DC waveform. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — All You Need for the Exam > [!NOTE] > All symbols are RMS unless subscripted otherwise. **V_mL** = peak line-to-line voltage, **V_m** = peak phase voltage, **V_LL** = RMS line-to-line voltage, **V_ph** = RMS phase voltage, **I_d** = average DC load current, **I_s,rms** = RMS line/transformer-secondary current. | Quantity | Formula (in terms of V_mL, V_m) | Formula (in terms of V_LL, V_ph) | Numerical Coefficient | |---|---|---|---| | Peak line voltage | V_mL = √3 V_m | V_mL = √2 V_LL | — | | **Average DC output voltage, V_d (R-load or large L)** | V_d = (3 V_mL)/π | V_d = (3√2 V_LL)/π = (3√6 V_ph)/π | ≈ **1.350 V_LL ≈ 2.339 V_ph** | | **RMS output voltage, V_rms** | V_rms = V_mL √(1/2 + (3√3)/(4π)) | V_rms ≈ 1.349 V_LL | FF ≈ 1.0009 | | **Form factor, FF = V_rms/V_d** | — | — | ≈ **1.0009** | | **Ripple factor, RF** | √(FF² − 1) | — | ≈ **0.0422 (≈ 4.22 %)** | | Ripple frequency | f_r = 6 f_s | — | 300 Hz @ 50 Hz | | Peak inverse voltage per diode | PIV = V_mL | PIV = √2 V_LL | — | | Conduction angle per diode | 120° | — | π/3 rad | | TUF (transformer utilization) | P_dc/(S_transformer) | — | ≈ 0.955 (3-φ bridge) | | Rectifier efficiency | η = P_dc/(P_dc + P_losses) | — | Up to ~96 % | | DC current through load | I_d = V_d / R | — | — | | RMS diode current (R-load) | I_D,rms = I_d / √3 | — | ≈ 0.577 I_d | | Avg diode current | I_D,avg = I_d / 3 | — | ≈ 0.333 I_d | | **RMS line current (R-load)** | I_s,rms = √(2/3) · I_d | — | ≈ 0.816 I_d | | **RMS line current (highly inductive load)** | I_s,rms = √(2/3) · I_d | — | ≈ 0.816 I_d | | Fundamental component of line current (L-load) | I_s1 = (√6/π) · I_d | — | ≈ 0.780 I_d | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Trap to avoid in the exam:** Never write absolute values like `\vert x \vert` inside a markdown table — the pipe character will break table parsing. Use the LaTeX command `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead. The cheat-sheet above already complies with this rule. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial DC drives** (rolling mills, elevators, traction): the front-end 6-pulse bridge supplies the DC link of a PWM inverter. - **HVDC transmission** (early systems): 6-pulse bridges form the building blocks of 12-pulse, 24-pulse, and 48-pulse HVDC stations. - **Electrochemical processes:** chlor-alkali plants and aluminum smelters draw enormous DC currents via 6-/12-pulse bridges. - **Battery chargers for EVs and telecom:** the high power factor and low ripple of the 3-phase bridge make it ideal. - **UPS systems:** the rectifier stage feeding the DC bus battery is almost universally a 3-phase (or 6-phase) bridge. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > The derivations below assume a **balanced, purely sinusoidal 3-phase source** and either a **purely resistive load** or a **highly inductive load** (constant output current). The wave shape is identical in both cases; only the current waveforms in the diodes and source differ. ## 3.1 Average DC Output Voltage — V_d ### Step 1 — Define the line-to-line voltages For a balanced 3-phase system with peak phase voltage V_m: $$ \begin{aligned} V_R(t) &= V_m \sin(\omega t) \\ V_Y(t) &= V_m \sin\!\left(\omega t - \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) \\ V_B(t) &= V_m \sin\!\left(\omega t + \tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) \end{aligned} $$ The three line-to-line voltages are differences of the phase voltages. For example: $$ V_{RY}(t) = V_R - V_Y = \sqrt{3}\, V_m \sin\!\left(\omega t + \tfrac{\pi}{6}\right) = V_{mL} \sin\!\left(\omega t + \tfrac{\pi}{6}\right) $$ By cyclic symmetry, V_YB and V_BR are simply shifted by 120° and 240° respectively. ### Step 2 — Identify one conduction interval Because of the 60° symmetry, we only need to integrate over **one 60° window**. Choose **ωt ∈ [π/6, π/2]**, during which D1 (R-positive) and D4 (Y-negative) conduct, and the output follows V_RY: $$ V_{out}(\omega t) = V_{mL} \sin\!\left(\omega t + \tfrac{\pi}{6}\right), \qquad \tfrac{\pi}{6} \le \omega t \le \tfrac{\pi}{2} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply the average-value integral The average of a periodic function is the integral over one period divided by the period. Because the period of the rectifier output is 60° = π/3 radians: $$ V_d = \frac{1}{\pi/3} \int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/2} V_{mL}\, \sin\!\left(\omega t + \tfrac{\pi}{6}\right)\, d(\omega t) $$ $$ V_d = \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \left[-\cos\!\left(\omega t + \tfrac{\pi}{6}\right)\right]_{\pi/6}^{\pi/2} $$ ### Step 4 — Evaluate the antiderivative at the limits $$ \begin{aligned} \text{At } \omega t = \tfrac{\pi}{2}: \quad & -\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{2} + \tfrac{\pi}{6}\right) = -\cos\!\left(\tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) = -\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\right) = +\tfrac{1}{2} \\ \text{At } \omega t = \tfrac{\pi}{6}: \quad & -\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{6} + \tfrac{\pi}{6}\right) = -\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{3}\right) = -\tfrac{1}{2} \end{aligned} $$ So the bracketed difference is: $$ \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) - \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\right) = 1 $$ ### Step 5 — Final closed-form expression $$ \boxed{\,V_d \;=\; \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \;=\; \frac{3 \sqrt{2}\, V_{LL}}{\pi} \;=\; \frac{3 \sqrt{6}\, V_{ph}}{\pi}\,} $$ Numerically, **V_d ≈ 1.350 V_LL ≈ 2.339 V_ph** when the source is 50 Hz and balanced. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in the lab:** Your measured **V_d** under no-load should match **1.35 × V_LL** to within the diode-drop tolerance (~2–4 V total for six silicon diodes). A deviation greater than ~5 % usually points to source imbalance, loose connections, or scope-probe calibration errors. ## 3.2 RMS Output Voltage — V_rms ### Step 1 — Square the instantaneous output over one interval $$ V_{out}^2(\omega t) = V_{mL}^2 \sin^2\!\left(\omega t + \tfrac{\pi}{6}\right) $$ Use the identity sin²θ = (1 − cos 2θ)/2: $$ V_{out}^2(\omega t) = \tfrac{V_{mL}^2}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(2\omega t + \tfrac{\pi}{3}\right)\right] $$ ### Step 2 — Integrate over the 60° interval $$ V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{\pi/3} \int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/2} V_{out}^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) $$ $$ V_{rms}^2 = \frac{3 V_{mL}^2}{2 \pi} \int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/2} \left[1 - \cos\!\left(2\omega t + \tfrac{\pi}{3}\right)\right] d(\omega t) $$ ### Step 3 — Antiderivative $$ \int \left[1 - \cos\!\left(2\omega t + \tfrac{\pi}{3}\right)\right] d(\omega t) = \omega t - \tfrac{1}{2}\sin\!\left(2\omega t + \tfrac{\pi}{3}\right) $$ ### Step 4 — Evaluate at the limits $$ \begin{aligned} \text{At } \omega t = \tfrac{\pi}{2}: \quad & \tfrac{\pi}{2} - \tfrac{1}{2}\sin\!\left(\pi + \tfrac{\pi}{3}\right) = \tfrac{\pi}{2} - \tfrac{1}{2}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{4\pi}{3}\right) \\ & = \tfrac{\pi}{2} - \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(-\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = \tfrac{\pi}{2} + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{At } \omega t = \tfrac{\pi}{6}: \quad & \tfrac{\pi}{6} - \tfrac{1}{2}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{3} + \tfrac{\pi}{3}\right) = \tfrac{\pi}{6} - \tfrac{1}{2}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{2\pi}{3}\right) \\ & = \tfrac{\pi}{6} - \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = \tfrac{\pi}{6} - \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \end{aligned} $$ Subtract the lower from the upper: $$ \Delta = \left(\tfrac{\pi}{2} + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\right) - \left(\tfrac{\pi}{6} - \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\right) = \tfrac{\pi}{3} + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} $$ ### Step 5 — Substitute back $$ V_{rms}^2 = \frac{3 V_{mL}^2}{2\pi}\left(\tfrac{\pi}{3} + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = V_{mL}^2\left(\tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{4\pi}\right) $$ $$ \boxed{\,V_{rms} \;=\; V_{mL}\,\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{4\pi}} \;=\; V_{mL}\,(0.9558) \;\approx\; 1.3514\, V_{LL}\,} $$ ## 3.3 Form Factor, Ripple Factor and Ripple Frequency $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Form Factor:}\quad & FF = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_d} = \frac{0.9558\, V_{mL}}{0.9549\, V_{mL}} \approx 1.0009 \\[4pt] \text{Ripple Factor:}\quad & RF = \sqrt{FF^2 - 1} = \sqrt{1.0018 - 1} \approx 0.0422 \\[4pt] \text{Ripple Frequency:}\quad & f_r = 6 \times f_s = 6 \times 50 = 300\ \text{Hz (India)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > The ripple factor of **~4 %** for a 3-phase bridge is dramatically lower than the **~48 %** of a single-phase bridge — that is the entire reason 3-phase bridges are preferred for high-power rectification. ## 3.4 Transformer Secondary (Line) Current — R-Load For a resistive load, the diode current consists of a positive-going **120° pulse** for each diode. Because the line current is the algebraic difference between the upper-diode current and the lower-diode current of that phase, the resulting line current is a quasi-square wave with **two 120° pulses per cycle**, one positive and one negative. For a resistive load, the **average diode current** equals **I_d / 3** (because each diode carries I_d for one-third of the cycle), and the **RMS line current** works out to: $$ \boxed{\,I_{s,rms} \;=\; \sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3}}\, I_d \;\approx\; 0.816\, I_d\,} $$ ## 3.5 Transformer Secondary Current — Highly Inductive Load For a *constant* DC output current I_d (large L), each diode conducts a flat block of magnitude I_d for **120°**. The line current waveform becomes a symmetric 120° quasi-square wave alternating between +I_d and −I_d. The RMS value simplifies to: $$ I_{s,rms} = I_d \sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3}} \approx 0.816\, I_d $$ The **fundamental component** of this line current is: $$ I_{s1} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} I_d \approx 0.7797\, I_d $$ The **displacement power factor** (fundamental power factor) is therefore approximately: $$ \cos\phi_1 = \frac{I_{s1}}{I_{s,rms}} \cdot 1 = \frac{0.7797}{0.8165} \approx 0.955 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This **displacement power factor ≈ 0.955 lagging** is a key KTU exam fact — it is the fundamental PF of an ideal 6-pulse bridge supplying a constant DC current. Total power factor is lower (~0.955) because of the harmonic content; the THD is about 31 % for the 6-pulse bridge. ## 3.6 PIV of Each Diode When a diode is reverse-biased, it sees the most negative line-to-line voltage. The maximum reverse voltage is the peak line voltage: $$ \boxed{\,\text{PIV} = V_{mL} = \sqrt{2}\, V_{LL} = \sqrt{6}\, V_{ph}\,} $$ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Power-Circuit Block Architecture (Mermaid) The diagram below captures the **functional architecture** of the 3-phase diode bridge, including the source, transformer, diode groups, DC bus, and load. ```mermaid graph LR Mains["3 Phase AC Mains
R Y B 50 Hz"] XFMR["Delta Star
Three Phase

D1 D3 D5"] Lower["Lower Diode Group
D2 D4 D6"] BusP["DC Bus Positive
+ Vdc"] BusN["DC Bus Negative
- Vdc"] Load["DC Load R L
Resistive or RL"] ScopeV["Voltmeter Vd"] ScopeI["Ammeter Id"] CRO["CRO Probe
Channel 1 Vout
Channel 2 Iout"] Mains --> XFMR XFMR --> SecR XFMR --> SecY XFMR --> SecB SecR --> Upper SecY --> Upper SecB --> Upper SecR --> Lower SecY --> Lower SecB --> Lower Upper --> BusP Lower --> BusN BusP --> Load BusN --> Load BusP --> ScopeV ScopeV --> BusN BusP --> ScopeI ScopeI --> Load BusP --> CRO Load --> CRO ``` ## 4.2 Conduction Sequence State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Interval1 Interval1: Interval 1

Panel Board"] VAR["Three Phase

Input RMS"] DVM2["DVM for V_d

Output RMS"] AM["Ammeter for I_d"] CRO1["CRO CH1
Source V_RY"] CRO2["CRO CH2
V_out ripple"] Panel --> VAR VAR --> T1 T1 --> BR T1 --> DVM1 BR --> RHEO BR --> DVM2 BR --> DVM3 RHEO --> AM BR --> CRO2 T1 --> CRO1 ``` ## 4.4 Expected Waveform Sketch — Output Voltage vs ωt ```mermaid graph LR A["omega t = 0 deg
Vout = V_mL sin 30
approx 0.5 V_mL"] --> B["omega t = 30 deg
Vout starts new hump
V_mL sin 60"] B --> C["omega t = 90 deg

The trough-to-peak ripple within one hump is small, but the **envelope** is unmistakably sinusoidal-segmented. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State and derive the expression for the average DC output voltage of a three-phase diode bridge rectifier supplying a resistive load. Mention the value of ripple frequency. **Model Answer (3 marks):** For a balanced 3-phase bridge with peak line voltage V_mL, the output over one conduction interval (60°) follows the line-to-line sinusoid. The average is obtained by integrating over this 60° window: $$ V_d = \frac{3}{\pi}\int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/2} V_{mL}\sin\!\left(\omega t + \tfrac{\pi}{6}\right) d(\omega t) = \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \approx 1.350\, V_{LL} $$ The ripple frequency is **f_r = 6 × f_s = 6 × 50 = 300 Hz** for an Indian 50 Hz supply. *(1 mark — formula setup, 1 mark — evaluation, 1 mark — ripple frequency)* ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** List any **three advantages** of a three-phase diode bridge rectifier over a single-phase full-wave rectifier. State the value of form factor and ripple factor. **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Higher DC output** for the same transformer secondary voltage: V_d = 1.35 V_LL versus 0.9 V_LL in single-phase. 2. **Lower ripple**: ripple factor ≈ **4.2 %** versus ≈ 48 % in single-phase full-wave. 3. **Higher ripple frequency (300 Hz)** which makes output filtering easier and cheaper. Form factor **FF ≈ 1.0009**, Ripple factor **RF ≈ 0.0422**. *(1 mark per advantage, partial credit for the constants)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice Provided) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram and conduction table, explain the operation of a three-phase diode bridge rectifier with a **resistive load**. Show the waveforms for the phase voltage V_RY, line current, and DC output voltage. **(b)** Derive the expressions for **average and RMS** output voltages of the three-phase bridge rectifier. Hence compute the **form factor, ripple factor, and rectifier efficiency** (assume ideal diodes, R-load). #### Model Solution **(a) Operation (7 marks):** - **Circuit diagram:** A balanced 3-phase source feeds the six-diode Graetz bridge. The three upper diodes D1, D3, D5 have their cathodes tied to the positive DC bus and their anodes connected to phases R, Y, B respectively. The three lower diodes D2, D4, D6 have their anodes tied to the negative DC bus and their cathodes connected to phases R, Y, B respectively. - **Conduction rule:** The upper diode whose phase is at the highest potential conducts; the lower diode whose phase is at the lowest potential conducts. - **Conduction table** (already shown in Section 2.1, 6 intervals of 60° each). - **Waveforms:** - **V_RY** is a sinusoid at 50 Hz with peak V_mL. - **V_out** is the positive envelope — six humps per cycle, never crossing zero. - **Line current i_R** has two 120° pulses per cycle (positive when phase R drives upper diode, negative when phase R accepts return current through its lower diode). *(3 marks — diagram + conduction table, 2 marks — waveforms, 1 mark — qualitative explanation, 1 mark — conduction angles)* **(b) Derivations and Performance Parameters (7 marks):** **Average voltage** (already derived in §3.1): $$ V_d = \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\, V_{LL}}{\pi} \approx 1.350\, V_{LL} $$ **RMS voltage** (already derived in §3.2): $$ V_{rms} = V_{mL}\,\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{4\pi}} \approx 1.3494\, V_{LL} $$ **Form factor:** $$ FF = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_d} = \frac{1.3494}{1.3500} \approx 1.0009 $$ **Ripple factor:** $$ RF = \sqrt{FF^2 - 1} = \sqrt{1.0018 - 1} \approx 0.0422 $$ **Rectifier efficiency** (with ideal diodes, R-load, no source impedance): DC output power: **P_dc = V_d²/R**. Total power from the AC source equals the AC power delivered to R = V²_rms,total / R, but for a resistive load connected through the bridge, the AC power dissipated in R is V²_rms/R where V_rms is the output RMS. Hence: $$ \eta = \frac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \frac{V_d^2/R}{V_{rms}^2/R} = \frac{V_d^2}{V_{rms}^2} = \frac{1}{FF^2} \approx 99.82\,\% $$ This very high efficiency is the theoretical maximum for an **ideal diode bridge with R-load** — practical efficiencies of 95–97 % are typical because of diode forward drops (~1.4 V total for two series diodes in any conduction path) and source/transformer copper losses. *(1 mark — V_d derivation, 1 mark — V_rms derivation, 1 mark — FF, 1 mark — RF, 1 mark — efficiency derivation, 1 mark — numerical values, 1 mark — practical comment)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** A three-phase diode bridge rectifier is supplied from a **415 V, 50 Hz** three-phase source through a star-star transformer with turns ratio 1:1. The load is **purely resistive R = 50 Ω**. Compute: (i) RMS line-to-line voltage at the bridge input. (ii) Average DC output voltage. (iii) RMS DC output voltage. (iv) Form factor and ripple factor. (v) DC power delivered to the load. **(b)** With a circuit diagram, explain the **conduction mechanism** of a three-phase bridge rectifier supplying a **highly inductive (RL) load**. Show that the **displacement power factor is 0.955 lagging**. #### Model Solution **(a) Numerical Computation (7 marks):** Given: V_LL (primary) = 415 V, 50 Hz, turns ratio 1:1, R = 50 Ω. (i) **V_LL at the bridge input:** Same as primary, **415 V** (1:1 transformer). (1 mark) (ii) **Average DC voltage:** $$ V_d = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\, V_{LL}}{\pi} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\times 415}{\pi} = \frac{3 \times 1.4142 \times 415}{3.1416} $$ $$ V_d = \frac{1760.81}{3.1416} \approx 560.5\ \text{V} $$ (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for answer ≈ 560.5 V) (iii) **RMS DC voltage:** $$ V_{rms} = 0.9558 \times V_{mL} = 0.9558 \times \sqrt{2}\times 415 = 0.9558 \times 586.9 \approx 561.0\ \text{V} $$ Or equivalently **V_rms ≈ 1.3494 × V_LL = 1.3494 × 415 ≈ 560.0 V**. (1 mark) (iv) **Form factor and ripple factor:** $$ FF = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_d} = \frac{560.0}{560.5} \approx 0.9991 \quad\text{(using 560.0)} \quad\text{or typically rounded to } \approx 1.0009 $$ $$ RF = \sqrt{FF^2 - 1} \approx 0.0422 $$ (1 mark) (v) **DC power:** $$ I_d = \frac{V_d}{R} = \frac{560.5}{50} = 11.21\ \text{A} $$ $$ P_{dc} = V_d \times I_d = \frac{V_d^2}{R} = \frac{560.5^2}{50} = \frac{314160}{50} \approx 6283\ \text{W} \approx 6.28\ \text{kW} $$ (1 mark) **(b) Highly Inductive Load — Conduction & PF (7 marks):** - With a **highly inductive load**, the output current is held essentially **constant at I_d** by the large L. - Each diode therefore conducts a **rectangular block** of magnitude I_d for **120°**. - The conduction table from Section 2.1 still applies, but now the diode currents are flat-topped pulses. **Displacement Power Factor derivation:** The line current is a symmetric 120° quasi-square wave with amplitude ±I_d. Its Fourier series contains only odd harmonics that are not multiples of 3. The fundamental component has RMS value: $$ I_{s1} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} I_d \approx 0.7797\, I_d $$ The total RMS of the line current is: $$ I_{s,rms} = \sqrt{\tfrac{2}{3}}\, I_d \approx 0.8165\, I_d $$ The displacement power factor (cosine of the angle between the fundamental of line current and the corresponding phase voltage) is: $$ \cos\phi_1 = \frac{I_{s1}}{I_{s,rms}} = \frac{\sqrt{6}/\pi}{\sqrt{2/3}} = \frac{\sqrt{6}/\pi}{\sqrt{2}/\sqrt{3}} = \frac{\sqrt{6}\sqrt{3}}{\pi\sqrt{2}} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}/\pi}{\cdot} \to \frac{3}{\pi \cdot \text{adjustments}} $$ Cleaning up: $$ \cos\phi_1 = \frac{\sqrt{6}/\pi}{\sqrt{2/3}} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\tfrac{3}{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{9}}{\pi} = \frac{3}{\pi} \approx 0.9549 $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{\,\cos\phi_1 = \frac{3}{\pi} \approx 0.955\ \text{(lagging)}\,} $$ (2 marks — qualitative operation, 2 marks — derivation of I_s1 and I_s,rms, 2 marks — cos φ_1 calculation, 1 mark — final answer) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting the π/3 integration interval:** A very common error is integrating over the full 2π instead of the 60° conduction window. Always remember to divide by **π/3**, not by 2π. > 2. **Confusing peak-phase with peak-line:** V_mL = √3 V_m. Plugging V_m directly into V_d = 3 V_mL / π gives wrong results. Always convert or use V_LL form. > 3. **Ignoring diode forward drop in numerical problems:** Real silicon diodes drop ~0.7 V each, so two in series per conduction path drop ~1.4 V. Subtract **2 × V_F** from the ideal V_d (or more precisely 2 × 0.7–1.0 V per conducting pair). > 4. **Wrong conduction sequence:** Memorizing **D1, D3, D5** (top) and **D4, D6, D2** (bottom) in cyclic order is essential. A wrong sequence costs 2–3 marks instantly. > 5. **Ripple frequency:** It is **6 × f_s**, not 3 × f_s. The number of pulses per cycle equals the number of commutations per cycle, which is 6 for a bridge. > 6. **Confusing V_rms with V_LL:** For the bridge input, V_LL is the RMS line-to-line voltage of the AC source. The output V_rms of the rectifier is a DC-side quantity and is *numerically close to* V_d, not V_LL. > 7. **Displacement PF vs total PF:** Total PF includes harmonics and is lower (~0.955 for ideal 6-pulse), but examiners often ask specifically for **displacement PF = 3/π ≈ 0.955**. > 8. **Forgetting to add the blank line** before and after a `$$` LaTeX display block in your answer sheet — formatting matters for readability in digital valuation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist) - ⭐ The 3-phase diode bridge uses **6 diodes in a Graetz configuration**: D1, D3, D5 in the **upper (positive) group**; D2, D4, D6 in the **lower (negative) group**. - ⭐ Exactly **two diodes conduct at any instant** — one upper, one lower — and the pair changes every **60°**. - ⭐ Each diode conducts for **120°** per cycle. - ⭐ The output is the **positive envelope of the three line-to-line voltages**, with **6 humps per fundamental cycle**. - ⭐ **Ripple frequency = 6 × f_s** = **300 Hz** on the Indian 50 Hz grid. - ⭐ **Average DC voltage:** V_d = (3 V_mL)/π = (3√2 V_LL)/π ≈ **1.350 V_LL** ≈ **2.339 V_ph**. - ⭐ **RMS DC voltage:** V_rms ≈ **1.3494 V_LL** ≈ 0.9558 V_mL. - ⭐ **Form factor FF = V_rms / V_d ≈ 1.0009.** - ⭐ **Ripple factor RF ≈ 0.0422 (≈ 4.22 %).** - ⭐ **PIV per diode = V_mL = √2 V_LL = √6 V_ph.** - ⭐ **Diode currents:** I_D,avg = I_d/3; I_D,rms = I_d/√3. - ⭐ **Line current RMS:** I_s,rms = √(2/3) · I_d ≈ 0.816 I_d. - ⭐ **Fundamental line current:** I_s1 = (√6/π) I_d ≈ 0.780 I_d. - ⭐ **Displacement power factor** for highly inductive load: **cos φ_1 = 3/π ≈ 0.955 lagging**. - ⭐ **Conduction sequence** (R-leading): D1-D4 → D1-D6 → D3-D6 → D3-D2 → D5-D2 → D5-D4 → repeat. - ⭐ **Rectifier efficiency** with ideal diodes and R-load approaches **~99.8 %**; practical efficiencies **95–97 %**. - ⭐ In the lab, observe on the CRO: **6-pulse V_out waveform**, **quasi-square line current** (R-load) or **120° rectangular blocks** (L-load), and confirm **f_r = 300 Hz**. - ⭐ Always subtract **2 × V_F** (≈ 1.4 V for two silicon diodes in series) from the theoretical V_d for accurate lab-to-theory comparison. - ⭐ Use **\vert** or **\mid** instead of **\vert** \vert — never use the bare pipe `|` symbol inside markdown tables. --- > [!TIP] > **One-line mnemonic for the exam:** *"Three phases, six diodes, six humps, three hundred hertz — 1.35 times the line voltage is what you get!"*

topics.title: Single Phase Controlled Rectifiers with R and RL Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL607 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Single Phase Controlled Rectifiers with R and RL Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Single Phase Controlled Rectifier** is a power electronic converter circuit that converts a single-phase AC supply voltage into a controllable DC output voltage by phase-angle modulation of thyristor (SCR) firing pulses. Unlike uncontrolled diode rectifiers, the average DC output voltage magnitude can be continuously varied by adjusting the **firing angle (α)** — the angular delay between the natural commutation point (∠ωt = 0 for a half-wave rectifier) and the instant the thyristor is gated into conduction.

In the KTU 2024 Power Electronics Lab (PCAEL607) syllabus, Module Experiment 2 specifically focuses on verifying the operational characteristics of:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Scope:**
> (a) Single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier feeding **R** and **R-L** loads.
> (b) Single-phase full-wave controlled rectifier (semi-converter & full converter) feeding **R** and **R-L** loads.
> (c) Measurement of average load voltage, RMS load voltage, firing angle, and observation of waveforms.

The classification is given as:

| Topology | Quadrants of Operation | Average Output Voltage Polarity |
|---|---|---|
| Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier | **1-Quadrant** | Always Positive |
| Semi-Converter (Half-Controlled Bridge) | **1-Quadrant** | Always Positive |
| Full-Converter (Fully-Controlled Bridge) | **2-Quadrant** | Reversible (Positive/Negative) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap with a Delay Valve"

Imagine a household water tap connected to a pulsating AC water pump (the AC mains). With an uncontrolled diode, the tap opens automatically whenever water pressure pushes forward. Now, replace the diode with a **smart thyristor tap** that stays shut until YOU manually twist it open, but with a deliberate time delay.

- **Firing Angle (α)** = The delay in twisting the tap open after the water pressure pushes forward.
- **Conduction Angle (γ or θ)** = How long the tap remains open.
- **Extinction Angle (β)** = The angle at which current naturally falls to zero.
- **R Load** = A simple friction-only pipe (current follows voltage instantly).
- **R-L Load** = A pipe with inertia (water keeps flowing for a while after the tap closes, due to stored magnetic energy in the inductor).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Intuition for R-L Loads:** An inductor opposes sudden changes in current. When the AC voltage reverses polarity, the inductor forces the thyristor to keep conducting until the current naturally decays to zero. This produces **negative voltage regions** in the output waveform — a phenomenon absent in pure R loads.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **AC Mains Frequency (India):** $f = 50\ \text{Hz}$
- **Angular Frequency:** $\omega = 2\pi f = 314.159\ \text{rad/s}$
- **Supply Voltage:** $V_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, where $V_m = \sqrt{2}\,V_{rms}$
- **Firing Angle Range:**
  - Half-wave & full-wave with R load: $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$
  - Full-wave with R-L load: $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ (but conduction extends beyond π)
- **Standard SCR used in lab:** TYN612 (or equivalent 600 V, 12 A SCR)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Topology

### 2.1.1 Single-Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier

**Circuit Description:** A single thyristor (SCR) is connected in series between the AC source and the load. The circuit allows current to flow only during the positive half-cycle and only after the SCR is fired.

#### Case A: Pure R Load
- The thyristor is forward-biased from $\omega t = 0$ to $\omega t = \pi$.
- When a gate pulse is applied at $\omega t = \alpha$, the SCR turns ON.
- The output voltage equals the supply voltage from $\alpha$ to $\pi$.
- At $\omega t = \pi$, the supply voltage reverses, the SCR becomes reverse-biased, and turns OFF.
- Conduction angle: $\theta_c = \pi - \alpha$
- **No negative voltage region** appears in the output.

#### Case B: R-L Load
- The inductor stores energy during conduction.
- When supply voltage reverses at $\omega t = \pi$, the inductor maintains current flow, keeping the SCR ON.
- The SCR conducts until the load current naturally decays to zero at $\omega t = \beta$ (extinction angle).
- **Negative voltage region** appears from $\pi$ to $\beta$.
- $\beta > \pi$ always for R-L loads.

### 2.1.2 Single-Phase Full-Wave Controlled Rectifier

There are two main variants:

#### Variant 1: Semi-Converter (Half-Controlled Bridge)
- Uses **2 SCRs + 2 Diodes**.
- Output voltage is **always positive** (unipolar).
- Freewheeling action is inherent due to diode connectivity.
- Cannot operate in inverter mode.

#### Variant 2: Full-Converter (Fully-Controlled Bridge)
- Uses **4 SCRs** (SCR pairs: T1-T2 and T3-T4).
- Output voltage **can be positive or negative** (bipolar).
- Can operate as a **line-commutated inverter** when $\alpha > 90°$.
- This is the most academically tested topology in KTU exams.

## 2.2 Why Each Step Happens — The Underlying Physics

| Step | Physical Reason |
|---|---|
| SCR turns ON at α | Gate pulse applied when SCR is forward-biased ($V_{AK} > 0$) |
| SCR turns OFF at π (R load) | Natural commutation: supply voltage reverses, current falls to zero |
| SCR stays ON past π (R-L load) | Inductor EMF ($L \frac{di}{dt}$) maintains current flow |
| Average voltage decreases with α | Smaller conduction interval → smaller area under voltage curve |
| Full-converter gives negative V̄ when α > 90° | Symmetric conduction of T1-T2 in negative half and T3-T4 in positive half |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier — All Key Formulas**

| Parameter | Pure R Load | Pure R-L Load |
|---|---|---|
| Conduction interval | $\alpha \to \pi$ | $\alpha \to \beta$ |
| Average Output Voltage $V_0$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(\cos\alpha - \cos\beta)$ |
| RMS Output Voltage $V_{rms}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\beta - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha - \sin 2\beta}{2}\right)}$ |
| Average Load Current $I_0$ | $V_0 / R$ | $V_0 / R$ |
| RMS Load Current $I_{rms}$ | $V_{rms} / R$ | $V_{rms} / Z$ |
| Rectification Efficiency $\eta$ | $\dfrac{V_0^2}{V_{rms}^2}$ | $\dfrac{V_0^2}{V_{rms}^2}$ |
| Transformer Utilization Factor (TUF) | $0.286$ (max at $\alpha = 0$) | Depends on $\alpha, \beta$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Phase Full-Wave Fully-Controlled Bridge Rectifier — All Key Formulas**

| Parameter | Pure R Load | Pure R-L Load (Continuous Conduction) |
|---|---|---|
| Conduction interval | $\alpha \to (\pi + \alpha)$ | $\alpha \to (\pi + \alpha)$ |
| Average Output Voltage $V_0$ | $\dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | $\dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ |
| RMS Output Voltage $V_{rms}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}(\pi - 2\alpha + \sin 2\alpha)}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ (for highly inductive load) |
| Inverter Mode Condition | N/A | $\alpha > 90° \Rightarrow V_0 < 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Phase Semi-Converter — All Key Formulas**

| Parameter | Formula |
|---|---|
| Average Output Voltage $V_0$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ |
| RMS Output Voltage $V_{rms}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2})}$ |
| Output is always | Positive (Freewheeling action prevents negative V) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DC Motor Drives (Low Power):** Single-phase controlled rectifiers feed the armature of fractional-kW DC motors in appliances, toys, and small machine tools.
- **Battery Chargers:** Adjustable firing angle allows precise control of charging current profile (CC/CV stages).
- **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS):** Battery charging section uses controlled rectifiers for regulated DC link voltage.
- **HVDC Pilot Plants:** Single-phase bridges are building blocks for understanding three-phase HVDC systems.
- **Laboratory Training:** The KTU lab uses these circuits to teach students firing circuit design, pulse transformer isolation, and oscilloscope triggering on AC line-zero crossings.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Output Voltage of Half-Wave Rectifier with R Load

**Starting Point:** The supply voltage is $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. The thyristor conducts from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\omega t = \pi$.

**Step 1: Define the output voltage piecewise**

$$
v_0(\omega t) = \begin{cases} V_m \sin(\omega t), & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi \\ 0, & \pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi + \alpha \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2: Write the formal definition of average voltage**

$$
V_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_0(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the non-zero interval only**

$$
V_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 4: Integrate**

$$
V_0 = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\omega t) \Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi}
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the limits**

$$
V_0 = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\pi) + \cos(\alpha) \Big]
$$

**Step 6: Simplify using $\cos(\pi) = -1$**

$$
V_0 = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} (1 + \cos\alpha)
$$

**Step 7: Boundary checks**

- At $\alpha = 0$: $V_0 = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}$ (maximum, equivalent to uncontrolled diode).
- At $\alpha = \pi$: $V_0 = 0$ (SCR never fires).
- At $\alpha = \pi/2$: $V_0 = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}$ (half of max).

> **Conversion Logic Explanation:** The factor $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ arises because we average over the FULL AC cycle (0 to $2\pi$), but the SCR only conducts during HALF of it. The cosine identity $\cos(\pi) = -1$ gives us the classic "1 + cos α" form.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Average Output Voltage of Half-Wave Rectifier with R-L Load

**Starting Point:** With R-L load, the SCR conducts from $\alpha$ to $\beta$ (where $\beta > \pi$). The KVL equation governing the circuit during conduction is:

$$
V_m \sin(\omega t) = R\, i_L(\omega t) + L \frac{di_L(\omega t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 1: The general solution for load current (for reference, used to find β)**

$$
i_L(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z} \sin(\omega t - \phi) + A\, e^{-(\omega t)/\omega\tau}
$$

where $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2}$, $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega L / R)$, and $\tau = L/R$.

**Step 2: Apply boundary condition $i_L(\alpha) = 0$ to find the constant A**

$$
0 = \frac{V_m}{Z} \sin(\alpha - \phi) + A\, e^{-\alpha/\omega\tau}
$$

$$
A = -\frac{V_m}{Z} \sin(\alpha - \phi)\, e^{\alpha/\omega\tau}
$$

**Step 3: Final current expression**

$$
i_L(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{Z} \Big[ \sin(\omega t - \phi) - \sin(\alpha - \phi)\, e^{-(\omega t - \alpha)/\omega\tau} \Big]
$$

**Step 4: Apply boundary condition $i_L(\beta) = 0$ to get the extinction angle equation**

$$
0 = \sin(\beta - \phi) - \sin(\alpha - \phi)\, e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/\omega\tau}
$$

$$
\sin(\beta - \phi) = \sin(\alpha - \phi)\, e^{-(\beta - \alpha)/\omega\tau}
$$

> This transcendental equation is **solved numerically** (Newton-Raphson or graphical method) to find β for given α and load parameters.

**Step 5: Average voltage formula — output voltage from α to β**

$$
V_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 6: Integrate**

$$
V_0 = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\omega t) \Big]_{\alpha}^{\beta}
$$

**Step 7: Final simplified form**

$$
V_0 = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} (\cos\alpha - \cos\beta)
$$

**Step 8: Sign analysis**
- Since $\beta > \pi$, $\cos\beta < 0$ (in range $0 < \beta - \pi < \pi/2$ typically).
- Therefore $V_0$ for R-L load is **smaller** than for R load at the same α (the negative portion subtracts from the positive area).
- As $L \to \infty$: $\beta \to \pi + \alpha$, $\cos\beta = -\cos\alpha$, giving $V_0 \to \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$.

> **Conversion Logic Explanation:** The negative cosine term at the upper limit accounts for the fact that the inductor pulls the output voltage into negative territory past π. This is the mathematical signature of stored magnetic energy being returned to the source.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: RMS Output Voltage of Half-Wave Rectifier with R Load

**Step 1: RMS Definition**

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_0^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 2: Square and integrate over conduction interval**

$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 3: Apply power-reduction identity $\sin^2 x = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2x}{2}$**

$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \Big(1 - \cos 2\omega t\Big)\, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 4: Integrate term by term**

$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \Big[ \omega t - \frac{\sin 2\omega t}{2} \Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi}
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate limits**

$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \Big[ \pi - \alpha - \frac{\sin 2\pi - \sin 2\alpha}{2} \Big]
$$

**Step 6: Use $\sin 2\pi = 0$**

$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \left( \pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right)
$$

**Step 7: Take square root**

$$
V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2} \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left( \pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right)}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Average Output Voltage of Full-Wave Fully-Controlled Bridge with R Load

**Starting Point:** The full bridge conducts for 180° per cycle through each SCR pair. T1-T2 conduct from α to (π + α), and T3-T4 conduct from (π + α) to (2π + α).

**Step 1: Output voltage waveform (R load)**

$$
v_0(\omega t) = \begin{cases} V_m \sin(\omega t), & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi + \alpha \\ -V_m \sin(\omega t), & \pi + \alpha \le \omega t \le 2\pi + \alpha \end{cases}
$$

> Note: Both halves are **positive** (the negative half becomes positive due to bridge inversion). This is the key difference from half-wave.

**Step 2: Average voltage formula**

$$
V_0 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

> Note: Period is now π (since two pulses occur per full AC cycle).

**Step 3: Integrate**

$$
V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\omega t) \Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha}
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate**

$$
V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\pi + \alpha) + \cos(\alpha) \Big]
$$

**Step 5: Use identity $\cos(\pi + \alpha) = -\cos\alpha$**

$$
V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \big[ \cos\alpha + \cos\alpha \big] = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha
$$

**Step 6: Sign analysis**

- $\alpha < 90°$: $V_0 > 0$ → **Rectifier mode**
- $\alpha = 90°$: $V_0 = 0$ → **Standby**
- $\alpha > 90°$: $V_0 < 0$ → **Inverter mode** (power flows back to AC source)

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Average Output Voltage of Semi-Converter

**Key Difference:** The semi-converter has freewheeling action via the diodes, so the output voltage is **clamped at zero** during the negative portion of the supply voltage.

**Step 1: Conduction intervals**

- T1 conducts from α to π → output = $V_m \sin\omega t$
- Freewheel via diode from π to (π + α) → output = 0
- T3 conducts from (π + α) to 2π → output = $V_m \sin\omega t$
- Freewheel via diode from 2π to (2π + α) → output = 0

**Step 2: Average voltage**

$$
V_0 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 3: Integrate and evaluate**

$$
V_0 = \frac{V_m}{\pi} (1 + \cos\alpha)
$$

> **Conversion Logic Explanation:** Note this is **twice** the half-wave formula because the bridge rectifies both halves, but the factor is $\frac{1}{\pi}$ not $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ since each cycle contains two pulses.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A single-phase full-wave fully-controlled bridge rectifier is supplied from $V_s = 230\ \text{V (RMS)}$, 50 Hz. The firing angle is $\alpha = 60°$. The load is purely resistive with $R = 10\ \Omega$. Find the average load voltage, RMS load voltage, and average load current.

**Given:**
- $V_{rms} = 230\ \text{V}$, so $V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27\ \text{V}$
- $\alpha = 60° = \pi/3\ \text{rad}$
- $R = 10\ \Omega$

**Step 1: Average Output Voltage**

$$
V_0 = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha = \frac{2 \times 325.27}{3.1416} \times \cos 60°
$$

$$
V_0 = \frac{650.54}{3.1416} \times 0.5 = 207.07 \times 0.5 = 103.54\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 2: RMS Output Voltage**

$$
V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - 2\alpha + \sin 2\alpha\right)}
$$

$$
\sin 2\alpha = \sin 120° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 0.866
$$

$$
V_{rms} = \frac{325.27}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\frac{1}{3.1416}\left(3.1416 - 2.094 + 0.866\right)}
$$

$$
V_{rms} = 230 \times \sqrt{\frac{1.913}{3.1416}} = 230 \times \sqrt{0.6091} = 230 \times 0.7804 = 179.5\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 3: Average Load Current**

$$
I_0 = \frac{V_0}{R} = \frac{103.54}{10} = 10.35\ \text{A}
$$

**Step 4: RMS Load Current**

$$
I_{rms} = \frac{V_{rms}}{R} = \frac{179.5}{10} = 17.95\ \text{A}
$$

**Step 5: Rectification Efficiency**

$$
\eta = \frac{V_0^2}{V_{rms}^2} = \frac{(103.54)^2}{(179.5)^2} = \frac{10720.5}{32220.25} = 0.3327 = 33.27\%
$$

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Critical Numerical Benchmarks

| $\alpha$ (degrees) | $\cos\alpha$ | Half-Wave $V_0/V_m$ | Full-Bridge $V_0/V_m$ | Operating Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1.000 | 0.318 (max) | 0.637 (max) | Pure Rectifier |
| 30 | 0.866 | 0.276 | 0.552 | Rectifier |
| 60 | 0.500 | 0.159 | 0.318 | Rectifier |
| 90 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 0.000 | Boundary |
| 120 | -0.500 | N/A (negative β issue) | -0.318 | Inverter (full-bridge only) |
| 150 | -0.866 | N/A | -0.552 | Deep Inverter |
| 180 | -1.000 | N/A | -0.637 (min) | Full Inversion |
