I_{1,peak} = \dfrac{V_{1,peak}}{R} = \dfrac{127.32}{10} = 12.732\ \text{A}
$$

$$
I_{1,rms} = \dfrac{90.03}{10} = 9.003\ \text{A}
$$

$$
I_{total,rms} = \dfrac{V_{o,rms}}{R} = \dfrac{100}{10} = 10\ \text{A}
$$

# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of MOSFET Inverter System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DCsrc["DC SOURCE VDC"] --> DCbus["DC BUS CAPACITOR BANK"]
    DCbus --> GateCkt["GATE DRIVE AND ISOLATION CIRCUIT"]
    GateCkt --> PWMgen["PWM CONTROLLER 555 OR DSP"]
    PWMgen -->|Gate Pulses| Hbridge["H BRIDGE 4 MOSFETs M1 M2 M3 M4"]
    DCbus -->|Supplies DC Link| Hbridge
    Hbridge -->|Switched Output| Filter["LC LOW PASS FILTER"]
    Filter --> Load["AC LOAD RESISTIVE OR INDUCTIVE"]
    Hbridge --> Snub["RC SNUBBER PROTECTION"]
    PWMgen --> Feedback["VOLTAGE AND CURRENT FEEDBACK SENSORS"]
    Load --> Feedback
    Feedback --> PWMgen
```

## 4.2 Full-Bridge MOSFET Inverter — Switching State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateA
    StateA : STATE A M1 and M2 ON Output equals plus VDC
    StateA --> StateB : After half period T by 2
    StateB : STATE B M3 and M4 ON Output equals minus VDC
    StateB --> StateA : After half period T by 2
    StateA : Freewheel Diode D3 D4
    StateB : Freewheel Diode D1 D2
    note right of StateA
        Positive half cycle:
        Current flows from
        plus VDC through M1,
        load, and M2 to GND
    end note
    note left of StateB
        Negative half cycle:
        Current flows from
        plus VDC through M3,
        load in reverse, and M4
    end note
```

## 4.3 Gate Signal Timing Sequence

```mermaid
gantt
    title MOSFET Gate Drive Timing for Full Bridge Inverter
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %s
    section M1 Gate
    M1 ON      :a1, 0, 10
    M1 OFF     :a2, 10, 10
    section M2 Gate
    M2 ON      :b1, 0, 10
    M2 OFF     :b2, 10, 10
    section M3 Gate
    M3 ON      :c1, 10, 10
    M3 OFF     :c2, 0, 10
    section M4 Gate
    M4 ON      :d1, 10, 10
    M4 OFF     :d2, 0, 10
    section Output V
    plus VDC   :e1, 0, 10
    minus VDC  :e2, 10, 10
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Timing Rule:** To avoid **shoot-through** (a short circuit across the DC bus), a **dead time** (typically 100 ns – 1 µs) must be inserted between turning OFF one MOSFET pair and turning ON the complementary pair. This is a guaranteed KTU viva question.

## 4.4 Functional Signal-Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL STAGE"]
        OSC["OSCILLATOR 50 HZ"]
        DRV["DEAD TIME GENERATOR"]
        ISO["OPTO ISOLATOR 4 CHANNEL"]
    end
    subgraph POWER["POWER STAGE"]
        DC["48 V DC BUS"]
        M1["M1 N CHANNEL MOSFET"]
        M2["M2 N CHANNEL MOSFET"]
        M3["M3 N CHANNEL MOSFET"]
        M4["M4 N CHANNEL MOSFET"]
        D1["D1 BODY DIODE"]
        D2["D2 BODY DIODE"]
        D3["D3 BODY DIODE"]
        D4["D4 BODY DIODE"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT STAGE"]
        LOAD["R L LOAD"]
        FILT["OPTIONAL LC FILTER"]
    end
    OSC --> DRV --> ISO
    ISO -->|Gate1| M1
    ISO -->|Gate2| M2
    ISO -->|Gate3| M3
    ISO -->|Gate4| M4
    DC --- M1
    M1 --- LOAD
    LOAD --- M2
    M2 --- DC
    DC --- M3
    M3 --- LOAD
    LOAD --- M4
    M4 --- DC
    LOAD --> FILT
```

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define an inverter. Why are MOSFETs preferred over BJTs in low-power inverter applications?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
An **inverter** is a power electronic converter that changes DC input voltage to a controlled AC output voltage of desired magnitude and frequency.

**MOSFETs are preferred over BJTs in low-power inverters because:**

- **Voltage-controlled device** → negligible gate drive current → simpler drive circuitry.
- **Higher switching speed** (ns range) → enables high-frequency PWM, reducing filter size.
- **No minority carrier storage** → no tail current → lower switching loss.
- **Positive temperature coefficient of $R_{DS(on)}$** → easy paralleling for higher current.
- **No secondary breakdown** → better ruggedness for inductive loads.

---

### **Q2. What is the role of anti-parallel diodes across each MOSFET in an H-bridge inverter?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The **anti-parallel diodes** (either the **intrinsic body diode** of the MOSFET or external fast-recovery diodes) provide a **freewheeling path** for the load current when the MOSFETs turn OFF.

- For an **inductive load**, current cannot change instantaneously. When MOSFETs switch OFF, the stored energy in the inductor forces current to continue flowing.
- Without diodes, this would cause a **large $L \cdot di/dt$ voltage spike** that could destroy the MOSFETs.
- The diodes clamp this voltage to the DC bus, **protecting the switches** and ensuring **commutation**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Choose this OR Question B*

#### **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a single-phase full-bridge square-wave MOSFET inverter. Derive the expression for its RMS output voltage. **(7 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit:** A full-bridge inverter has four MOSFETs (M1, M2, M3, M4) connected across a DC source $V_{DC}$ with the load between the midpoints of the two legs.

**Working Principle (Step-by-Step):**

**Mode 1 (0 to T/2):** MOSFETs **M1 and M2 are ON**; M3 and M4 are OFF.
- Current path: $V_{DC} \rightarrow M1 \rightarrow \text{Load} \rightarrow M2 \rightarrow \text{Ground}$.
- Output voltage $v_o = +V_{DC}$.

**Mode 2 (T/2 to T):** MOSFETs **M3 and M4 are ON**; M1 and M2 are OFF.
- Current path: $V_{DC} \rightarrow M3 \rightarrow \text{Load (reverse)} \rightarrow M4 \rightarrow \text{Ground}$.
- Output voltage $v_o = -V_{DC}$.

The result is a **symmetrical square wave** of amplitude $\pm V_{DC}$ and frequency $f_o = 1/T$.

**RMS Derivation:**

$$
V_{rms}^2 = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v_o^2 \, dt = \dfrac{1}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T/2} V_{DC}^2 \, dt + \int_{T/2}^{T} V_{DC}^2 \, dt \right] = V_{DC}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{rms} = V_{DC}}
$$

---

#### **(b)** A single-phase full-bridge MOSFET inverter is supplied from a **$V_{DC} = 220$ V** DC bus. It feeds a purely resistive load of $R = 20\ \Omega$ with a 50 Hz square-wave output. Calculate: **(i)** Output RMS voltage, **(ii)** Peak fundamental component, **(iii)** Fundamental RMS voltage, **(iv)** Total Harmonic Distortion (THD), and **(v)** Total RMS load current. **(7 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Output RMS Voltage:**

$$
V_{o,rms} = V_{DC} = 220\ \text{V}
$$

**(ii) Peak Fundamental Component:**

$$
V_{1,peak} = \dfrac{4 V_{DC}}{\pi} = \dfrac{4 \times 220}{\pi} = 280.11\ \text{V}
$$

**(iii) Fundamental RMS Voltage:**

$$
V_{1,rms} = \dfrac{V_{1,peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{280.11}{1.4142} = 198.05\ \text{V}
$$

**(iv) Total Harmonic Distortion:**

$$
THD = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{V_{o,rms}}{V_{1,rms}}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{220}{198.05}\right)^2 - 1}
$$

$$
THD = \sqrt{1.2339 - 1} = \sqrt{0.2339} \approx 0.4836 = 48.36\%
$$

**(v) Total RMS Load Current:**

$$
I_{total,rms} = \dfrac{V_{o,rms}}{R} = \dfrac{220}{20} = 11\ \text{A}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

#### **(a)** Explain the **half-bridge inverter** using MOSFETs with a circuit diagram. Compare it with the **full-bridge inverter** in terms of voltage utilization, component count, and applications. **(7 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Half-Bridge Inverter Circuit:**
- Two MOSFETs (M1, M2) in series across the DC bus.
- Two **equal-value capacitors** ($C_1 = C_2$) in series, forming a **virtual midpoint (N)**.
- Load is connected between the **MOSFET midpoint (A)** and **capacitor midpoint (N)**.

**Working:**

**Mode 1:** M1 ON, M2 OFF → $V_A = +V_{DC}/2$ → Load voltage = $+V_{DC}/2$.

**Mode 2:** M1 OFF, M2 ON → $V_A = -V_{DC}/2$ → Load voltage = $-V_{DC}/2$.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Half-Bridge | Full-Bridge |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| MOSFETs Required | 2 | 4 |
| Capacitors Required | 2 (large, equal) | 0 |
| Output Peak Voltage | $V_{DC}/2$ | $V_{DC}$ |
| DC Bus Utilization | 50% | 100% |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Applications | Low-power UPS, small fans | Solar inverters, motor drives |

---

#### **(b)** A single-phase **half-bridge MOSFET inverter** is fed from $V_{DC} = 200\ \text{V}$. It produces a 50 Hz square wave across a resistive load of $R = 25\ \Omega$. Determine: **(i)** Output peak voltage, **(ii)** Output RMS voltage, **(iii)** Fundamental peak voltage, and **(iv)** Load fundamental RMS current. Also explain why the output of this inverter **cannot be directly connected to the utility grid**. **(7 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Output Peak Voltage:**

$$
V_{o,peak} = \dfrac{V_{DC}}{2} = \dfrac{200}{2} = 100\ \text{V}
$$

**(ii) Output RMS Voltage (Square Wave):**

$$
V_{o,rms} = \dfrac{V_{DC}}{2} = 100\ \text{V}
$$

**(iii) Fundamental Peak Voltage (Half-Bridge):**

For half-bridge, the fundamental peak is half that of a full-bridge:

$$
V_{1,peak} = \dfrac{2 V_{DC}}{\pi} = \dfrac{2 \times 200}{\pi} = 127.32\ \text{V}
$$

**(iv) Fundamental RMS Current:**

$$
V_{1,rms} = \dfrac{V_{1,peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{127.32}{1.4142} = 90.03\ \text{V}
$$

$$
I_{1,rms} = \dfrac{V_{1,rms}}{R} = \dfrac{90.03}{25} = 3.60\ \text{A}
$$

**Why this cannot connect directly to the grid:**

A **square-wave inverter output contains all odd harmonics** (3rd, 5th, 7th…) with substantial amplitude. Direct grid connection would inject harmonic currents into the power system, causing:

- **Excessive heating** in transformers and cables.
- **Resonance** with grid inductances and capacitances.
- **Malfunction** of sensitive electronic equipment.
- **Violation** of IEEE 519 harmonic standards.

Therefore, an **LC filter** and/or **PWM-based sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)** control must be used to make the output near-sinusoidal before grid connection.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing peak and RMS:** Many students write $V_{o,rms} = V_{DC}/\sqrt{2}$ — this is wrong for a square wave (only true for sine). For square wave, $V_{o,rms} = V_{DC}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2** between half-bridge and full-bridge when comparing fundamental components.
> 3. **Not drawing anti-parallel diodes** in the H-bridge — full 1 mark lost in circuit diagrams.
> 4. **Skipping the dead time** mention in the timing diagram — examiners specifically look for this.
> 5. **No explanation of shoot-through** in working — a favorite 2-mark question.
> 6. **Wrong THD formula** — students often write $THD = V_{harmonic}/V_{fundamental}$ instead of the square root of sum-of-squares form.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

> 📌 **Quick Revision Checklist (Read before entering the exam hall)**

- ✅ An **inverter** converts **DC → AC**; MOSFET-based inverters are **Voltage-Source Inverters (VSI)**.
- ✅ Three common topologies: **Push-Pull, Half-Bridge, Full-Bridge (H-Bridge)**.
- ✅ Full-Bridge output swings between $+V_{DC}$ and $-V_{DC}$; Half-Bridge swings between $\pm V_{DC}/2$.
- ✅ Square-wave output: $V_{o,rms} = V_{DC}$ (full-bridge), $V_{o,rms} = V_{DC}/2$ (half-bridge).
- ✅ Fundamental peak (full-bridge): $V_{1,peak} = 4V_{DC}/\pi$.
- ✅ Fundamental peak (half-bridge): $V_{1,peak} = 2V_{DC}/\pi$.
- ✅ Square-wave THD is fixed at **48.34%** — this is why PWM is essential.
- ✅ Only **odd harmonics** (1, 3, 5, 7, …) are present in a square wave.
- ✅ The $n^{th}$ harmonic amplitude is $V_{n,peak} = 4V_{DC}/(n\pi)$.
- ✅ **Anti-parallel (body) diodes** are mandatory for inductive load freewheeling.
- ✅ **Dead time** (typically 100 ns – 1 µs) must be inserted between complementary MOSFET switching to prevent **shoot-through**.
- ✅ MOSFET is a **voltage-controlled, unipolar, majority-carrier** device — gate voltage ($V_{GS}$) controls drain current ($I_D$).
- ✅ Conduction loss: $P_{cond} = I_{D,rms}^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$; Switching loss increases with $f_s$.
- ✅ **Grid connection requires filtering** (LC filter) or **PWM** to reduce THD to acceptable levels (typically < 5%).
- ✅ Lab experiment typically uses **IRF540, IRF840, or similar N-channel power MOSFETs** with gate drive from **555 timer, SG3524, or Arduino/STM32 PWM**.

---

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Switched-Mode Power Supply (SMPS):** A Switched-Mode Power Supply is an electronic power conversion circuit that uses an actively controlled semiconductor switching device (typically a **MOSFET**, **IGBT**, or **BJT**) operating in the **ON–OFF (cut-off/saturation)** region, in conjunction with **magnetic energy-storage elements** (inductors and high-frequency transformers) and **filter capacitors**, to convert an unregulated DC or AC input voltage into one or more regulated DC output voltages with **high efficiency (typically 80%–95%)**.

In the strict KTU 2024 lab-viva sense, the **realization of a simple SMPS** refers to the physical hardware assembly and functional verification of a closed-loop DC–DC converter (commonly a **Buck**, **Boost**, or **Buck–Boost** topology, or an isolated **Flyback** topology) employing a **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) controller IC** (e.g., SG3525, UC3842, TL494, or NE555-based analog PWM) for regulation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Tap" Intuition

Imagine you are filling a water tank whose inlet is a high-pressure pipeline.

- A **linear regulator** is like **leaving the tap fully open and then squeezing the pipe** to control flow. The energy lost in "squeezing" (the pressure drop across a series pass element) turns into heat. The tank fills, but most of the water energy is wasted.
- An **SMPS** is like **opening and closing the tap very fast** (ON/OFF pulses). The average flow over time is controlled by **how long the tap stays open in each cycle** (the *duty cycle*). The leftover pressure is either *stored* in a small **expansion tank (capacitor)** or *redirected* into a *secondary pipe (transformer/inductor)* and recycled.

This rapid switching at **high frequencies (20 kHz to 1 MHz)** allows the use of **very small magnetic components**, which is why SMPS units are compact and efficient compared to bulky 50 Hz linear supplies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metrics in SMPS Realization:**
> - **Efficiency ($\eta$):** $\geq 80\%$ (Linear $\leq 50\%$)
> - **Switching Frequency ($f_s$):** $20 \text{ kHz} \rightarrow 1 \text{ MHz}$
> - **Line Regulation:** $\leq 0.1\%$
> - **Load Regulation:** $\leq 0.5\%$

---

## 1.3 Classification of SMPS (As Recognized in KTU Lab Syllabus)

| SMPS Class | Conversion | Isolation | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Buck Converter** | DC $\rightarrow$ lower DC | Non-isolated | Laptop CPU rails |
| **Boost Converter** | DC $\rightarrow$ higher DC | Non-isolated | Power-factor correction, LED drivers |
| **Buck-Boost** | DC $\rightarrow$ inverted/lower or higher | Non-isolated | Battery-powered portable devices |
| **Flyback** | DC $\rightarrow$ isolated DC | Isolated (HF Transformer) | Mobile chargers, $< 150 \text{ W}$ adapters |
| **Forward Converter** | DC $\rightarrow$ isolated DC | Isolated | $150 \text{ W} \rightarrow 500 \text{ W}$ |
| **Half/Full-Bridge** | DC $\rightarrow$ isolated DC | Isolated | High-power industrial SMPS |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Typical SMPS

A generalized SMPS realization (whether isolated or non-isolated) follows the **eight-block canonical signal-flow**:

1. **EMI/RFI Input Filter** — Suppresses conducted noise from entering the AC mains.
2. **Input Bridge Rectifier + Bulk Filter Capacitor** — Converts AC mains (or unregulated DC) to a raw, ripple-containing DC link (typically $V_{in} \approx 1.414 \times V_{RMS}$).
3. **PWM Controller (Reference + Error Amplifier + Sawtooth Generator + Comparator)** — Generates the gate-drive signal whose **duty cycle $D$** is dynamically adjusted.
4. **Power Semiconductor Switch ($S$)** — **MOSFET/IGBT** chops the DC link at $f_s$.
5. **Energy-Storage / Transfer Element** — Inductor $L$ (and/or HF transformer).
6. **Output Rectifier (Freewheeling Diode $D_f$ + secondary diode)** — Recovers the chopped energy.
7. **Output LC Filter** — Smooths the chopped waveform into a clean DC output.
8. **Feedback Network (Optocoupler + TL431 shunt reference)** — Senses $V_{out}$, compares with $V_{ref}$, modulates $D$.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — The "Why" Behind Each Block

- **Why a switch and not a rheostat?** A switch dissipates $P = V \cdot I$ only during the *transition* intervals. When fully ON, $V_{DS} \approx 0$ (low loss); when fully OFF, $I_D = 0$ (no loss). Thus average loss is small.
- **Why a high-frequency transformer (if isolated)?** Per Faraday's law, $V = N \cdot \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$, so at $f_s = 50 \text{ kHz}$ the core cross-section $A_c$ shrinks by a factor of $1000$ compared to a 50 Hz transformer. This is why SMPS adapters fit in the palm.
- **Why PWM and not PFM?** Pulse-Width Modulation keeps $f_s$ constant, allowing the LC filter to be designed for a single pole. Simpler, lower-ripple output.
- **Why a feedback loop?** To compensate for input voltage variations (line regulation) and load current variations (load regulation).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **highest-yield formulas** for the KTU ESE. Memorize the duty-cycle relationships.

| # | Topology | Output Voltage $V_o$ | Inductor Ripple $\Delta I_L$ | Critical Inductance $L_{crit}$ | Output Cap. $C_{min}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Buck** | $D \cdot V_{in}$ | $\dfrac{V_o(1-D)}{f_s L}$ | $\dfrac{(1-D)R}{2 f_s}$ | $\dfrac{(1-D)}{8 L f_s^2 \cdot \Delta V_o / V_o}$ |
| 2 | **Boost** | $\dfrac{V_{in}}{1-D}$ | $\dfrac{V_o D (1-D)}{f_s L}$ | $\dfrac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$ | $\dfrac{D}{R f_s \cdot \Delta V_o / V_o}$ |
| 3 | **Buck-Boost** | $-\dfrac{D \cdot V_{in}}{1-D}$ (inverted) | $\dfrac{V_o(1-D)}{f_s L}$ | $\dfrac{(1-D)^2 R}{2 D f_s}$ | $\dfrac{D}{R f_s \cdot \Delta V_o / V_o}$ |
| 4 | **Flyback (Isolated)** | $D \cdot V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(1-D)}$ | — | Magnetizing: $L_m = \dfrac{V_{in} D}{2 I_{pk} f_s}$ | $\dfrac{I_o D}{f_s \cdot \Delta V_o}$ |

| # | Cross-Cutting Formula | Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | **Duty Cycle (Buck)** | $D = \dfrac{V_o}{V_{in}}$ | Ratio of switch ON-time to total period $T_s$ |
| 6 | **Duty Cycle (Boost)** | $D = 1 - \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_o}$ | Same converter family, different relationship |
| 7 | **Power Loss in Switch** | $P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{in} I_o (t_r + t_f) f_s$ | Switching loss dominates at high $f_s$ |
| 8 | **Conduction Loss** | $P_{cond} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ | Resistive heating during ON-state |
| 9 | **Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Always quote with $\geq 80\%$ target |
| 10 | **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)** | $I_L \geq 0$ throughout $T_s$ | Inductor current never touches zero |
| 11 | **Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM)** | $I_L = 0$ for some interval | Occurs at light loads |

> **Note on Table Delimiters:** All absolute-value expressions such as $\vert D \vert$ and $\vert 1-D \vert$ in the table are written using the `\vert` LaTeX command to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe parser.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

SMPS units power virtually every modern electronic load: **laptop adapters, mobile-phone chargers (5 V/2 A), server SMPS (12 V/50 A), telecom 48 V bus converters, EV traction inverters, satellite power conditioning units, and aircraft avionics**. The lab realization trains students in the canonical engineering workflow: **specification $\rightarrow$ topology selection $\rightarrow$ component selection $\rightarrow$ duty-cycle calculation $\rightarrow$ magnetics design $\rightarrow$ PWM control $\rightarrow$ closed-loop regulation testing**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 ESE often asks for the **derivation of the DC voltage transfer function** for a Buck or Boost converter under steady-state CCM, using **volt-second balance** on the inductor. The full derivation is reproduced below.

## 3.1 Derivation — Output Voltage of a Buck Converter

### Step 1 — Define the Two Switching Intervals

During one switching period $T_s = 1/f_s$, the MOSFET switch $S$ is:
- **ON** for $t_{on} = D \cdot T_s$
- **OFF** for $t_{off} = (1-D) \cdot T_s$

where $D$ is the duty cycle, $0 \leq D \leq 1$.

### Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) During Switch-ON

When $S$ is ON, the diode $D_f$ is reverse-biased (OFF). The inductor $L$ is connected directly across the input minus the output:

$$ v_L(t) = V_{in} - V_o $$

### Step 3 — Apply KVL During Switch-OFF

When $S$ is OFF, the inductor current freewheels through $D_f$. The voltage across $L$ reverses polarity:

$$ v_L(t) = - V_o $$

### Step 4 — Apply Volt-Second Balance (Steady-State Condition)

In **steady-state CCM**, the average voltage across the inductor over one full cycle must be zero (otherwise the inductor current would ramp up or down indefinitely). This is the **volt-second balance** principle:

$$ \langle v_L \rangle = 0 $$

$$ \int_{0}^{T_s} v_L(t) \, dt = (V_{in} - V_o) D T_s + (-V_o)(1-D) T_s = 0 $$

### Step 5 — Algebraic Simplification

Expanding the bracketed term:

$$ (V_{in} - V_o) D T_s - V_o (1-D) T_s = 0 $$

Divide through by $T_s$ (non-zero):

$$ (V_{in} - V_o) D - V_o (1-D) = 0 $$

$$ V_{in} D - V_o D - V_o + V_o D = 0 $$

$$ V_{in} D - V_o = 0 $$

### Step 6 — Final Result

$$ \boxed{\, V_o = D \cdot V_{in} \,} $$

> This is the **fundamental Buck-converter relationship**. It is *linear* in $D$, which makes the Buck the most intuitive topology to realize in the lab.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Output Voltage of a Boost Converter

### Step 1 — Switch-ON Interval (Inductor Charges)

When $S$ is ON, the input source charges the inductor $L$ through the switch. The diode $D_f$ is reverse-biased. The output capacitor $C$ alone supplies the load:

$$ v_L(t) = V_{in} $$

### Step 2 — Switch-OFF Interval (Inductor Discharges to Load)

When $S$ is OFF, the inductor voltage adds to $V_{in}$ and forward-biases the diode, charging $C$ and feeding the load:

$$ v_L(t) = V_{in} - V_o $$

### Step 3 — Volt-Second Balance

$$ V_{in} \cdot D \cdot T_s + (V_{in} - V_o)(1-D) T_s = 0 $$

### Step 4 — Expand and Simplify

$$ V_{in} D + V_{in}(1-D) - V_o(1-D) = 0 $$

$$ V_{in}[D + (1-D)] - V_o(1-D) = 0 $$

$$ V_{in} - V_o(1-D) = 0 $$

### Step 5 — Final Result

$$ \boxed{\, V_o = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1 - D} \,} $$

> As $D \rightarrow 1$, $V_o \rightarrow \infty$ (theoretically). In practice, parasitic resistances bound the output, which is why Boost converters are always operated with $D < 0.85$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** Design a Buck SMPS to deliver $V_o = 12 \text{ V}$ from $V_{in} = 24 \text{ V}$ with load $R = 10 \text{ }\Omega$. The switching frequency is $f_s = 50 \text{ kHz}$. Choose $L$ such that the inductor ripple $\Delta I_L$ is $20\%$ of the average inductor current.

**Step 1 — Compute the duty cycle:**

$$ D = \dfrac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{12}{24} = 0.5 $$

**Step 2 — Compute the average load current:**

$$ I_o = \dfrac{V_o}{R} = \dfrac{12}{10} = 1.2 \text{ A} $$

**Step 3 — Average inductor current (Buck, CCM):**

$$ I_L = I_o = 1.2 \text{ A} $$

**Step 4 — Allowable ripple:**

$$ \Delta I_L = 0.2 \times 1.2 = 0.24 \text{ A} $$

**Step 5 — Solve the ripple equation for $L$:**

$$ \Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_o (1-D)}{f_s L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad L = \dfrac{V_o (1-D)}{f_s \cdot \Delta I_L} $$

$$ L = \dfrac{12 \times (1 - 0.5)}{50 \times 10^3 \times 0.24} $$

$$ L = \dfrac{6}{12000} $$

$$ \boxed{\, L = 500 \text{ }\mu\text{H} \,} $$

**Step 6 — Choose output capacitor for $\Delta V_o / V_o = 1\%$ ripple:**

$$ C = \dfrac{1-D}{8 L f_s^2 \cdot (\Delta V_o / V_o)} = \dfrac{0.5}{8 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} \times (50 \times 10^3)^2 \times 0.01} $$

$$ C = \dfrac{0.5}{8 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.5 \times 10^9 \times 0.01} $$

$$ C = \dfrac{0.5}{100000} $$

$$ \boxed{\, C = 5 \text{ }\mu\text{F} \,} $$

In practice, a $47 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ low-ESR electrolytic is chosen to provide design margin.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete SMPS Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mains[AC Mains 230V 50Hz] --> emi[EMI Input Filter]
    emi --> rect[Bridge Rectifier + Bulk Capacitor]
    rect --> dclink[Unregulated DC Link]
    dclink --> pwm[PWM Controller SG3525]
    pwm --> drv[Gate Driver]
    drv --> mos[Power MOSFET Switch]
    mos --> mag[Inductor L / HF Transformer]
    mag --> fr[Freewheel Diode + Output Rectifier]
    fr --> lcf[Output LC Filter]
    lcf --> out[Regulated DC Output Vout]
    out --> fb[Feedback Optocoupler + TL431]
    fb --> pwm
    pwm -.-> saw[Sawtooth Reference]
    saw -.-> pwm
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Buck-Converter Realization Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    vin[Vin] --> mos
    mos[Power MOSFET S] --> nodeA[Switching Node]
    nodeA --> ind[Inductor L]
    ind --> vout[Vout]
    vout --> cap[Capacitor C]
    cap --> gnd1[Ground]
    nodeA --> dio[Freewheeling Diode Df]
    dio --> gnd2[Ground]
    vout --> rload[Load R]
    rload --> gnd3[Ground]
    ctrl[PWM Control Signal] --> mos
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Closed-Loop Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref[Vref 2.5V TL431] --> err[Error Amplifier]
    fb[Sensed Vout] --> err
    err --> comp[Comparator with Sawtooth]
    saw[Oscillator fs] --> comp
    comp --> gate[Gate Drive to MOSFET]
    gate --> plant[Power Stage Buck Boost Flyback]
    plant --> outnode[Output Vout]
    outnode --> fb
    plant --> sensor[Current Sense Resistor]
    sensor --> comp
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a block diagram instead of a physical circuit drawing?**
> Per the V10 protocol safeguard, the physical circuit would require dozens of components. The block-level functional architecture is the **industry-standard** representation used in datasheets (e.g., TI UCC28C42, Power Integrations TOP switch) and is the same representation expected in the KTU lab record.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define SMPS. Why is it preferred over linear regulators?**

**Model Answer (8–10 lines):**

A **Switched-Mode Power Supply (SMPS)** is a power-conversion circuit that uses a semiconductor switch (MOSFET/IGBT) operating in ON/OFF mode at high frequency, in conjunction with energy-storage elements (inductors, capacitors, and high-frequency transformers), to convert an unregulated input to a regulated DC output.

**Advantages over linear regulators:**

- **High efficiency ($80\% \rightarrow 95\%$)** because the switch dissipates negligible power in ON/OFF states. A linear regulator, in contrast, drops the excess voltage across a pass transistor, dissipating $P = (V_{in} - V_o) \cdot I_L$ as heat.
- **Compact size** because high-frequency operation ($f_s = 20 \text{ kHz} \rightarrow 1 \text{ MHz}$) shrinks the magnetic components by orders of magnitude.
- **Electrical isolation** is easily achieved using a high-frequency transformer.
- **Flexible topologies** — Buck, Boost, Flyback, Forward, Half-Bridge, Full-Bridge — adapt to nearly any voltage-conversion requirement.

(3 marks: definition 1, comparison 1, advantages 1)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of a PWM controller in an SMPS? Name two commonly used PWM controller ICs.**

**Model Answer (6–8 lines):**

A **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) controller** is the brain of the SMPS. It samples the output voltage through a feedback network, compares it to an internal reference voltage, and modulates the **duty cycle $D$** of the gate-drive signal sent to the power switch. When the output voltage falls below the setpoint, $D$ is increased to deliver more energy; when it rises above, $D$ is reduced.

**Two commonly used PWM controller ICs:**

1. **SG3525** — Industry-standard, current-mode or voltage-mode, $f_s$ up to 400 kHz.
2. **UC3842** — Current-mode controller widely used in offline flyback converters.
3. (Acceptable alternatives: **TL494**, **NE555-based analog PWM**, **UC3843**.)

(3 marks: role 1, ICs 1, explanation 1)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **Buck Converter** operating in continuous conduction mode (CCM). Derive the expression for the output voltage in terms of the duty cycle. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Design a Buck SMPS with the following specifications: $V_{in} = 30 \text{ V}$, $V_o = 15 \text{ V}$, $I_o = 2 \text{ A}$, switching frequency $f_s = 25 \text{ kHz}$, and allowable inductor current ripple $\Delta I_L = 0.4 \text{ A}$. Compute the values of $L$ and $C$ for an output voltage ripple of $1\%$. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Working of Buck Converter + Derivation

**Circuit Description:**

A Buck converter consists of:
- A **power switch $S$** (MOSFET) connected between $V_{in}$ and a switching node.
- An **inductor $L$** between the switching node and the output.
- A **freewheeling diode $D_f$** from the switching node to ground.
- A **filter capacitor $C$** in parallel with the load $R$.

**Mode 1 — Switch ON (duration $DT_s$):**
Current flows from $V_{in}$ through $L$ to the load. The diode is reverse-biased. The inductor stores energy, and its current ramps up linearly. KVL gives $v_L = V_{in} - V_o$ (positive, so $i_L$ rises).

**Mode 2 — Switch OFF (duration $(1-D)T_s$):**
The inductor maintains current flow through the freewheeling diode. KVL gives $v_L = -V_o$ (negative, so $i_L$ falls).

**Derivation (volt-second balance):**

$$ V_{in} D T_s + (-V_o)(1-D) T_s = 0 $$

$$ V_{in} D - V_o (1-D) = 0 $$

$$ \boxed{\, V_o = D \cdot V_{in} \,} $$

(Marks: circuit 2, modes 2, derivation 3)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Numerical Design

**Given:** $V_{in} = 30 \text{ V}$, $V_o = 15 \text{ V}$, $I_o = 2 \text{ A}$, $f_s = 25 \text{ kHz}$, $\Delta I_L = 0.4 \text{ A}$, $\Delta V_o / V_o = 0.01$.

**Step 1 — Duty cycle:**

$$ D = \dfrac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{15}{30} = 0.5 $$

**Step 2 — Inductor value:**

$$ L = \dfrac{V_o (1-D)}{f_s \cdot \Delta I_L} = \dfrac{15 \times 0.5}{25 \times 10^3 \times 0.4} $$

$$ L = \dfrac{7.5}{10000} $$

$$ \boxed{\, L = 750 \text{ }\mu\text{H} \,} $$

**Step 3 — Output capacitor:**

$$ C = \dfrac{1-D}{8 L f_s^2 \cdot (\Delta V_o/V_o)} $$

$$ C = \dfrac{0.5}{8 \times 750 \times 10^{-6} \times (25 \times 10^3)^2 \times 0.01} $$

$$ C = \dfrac{0.5}{8 \times 750 \times 10^{-6} \times 6.25 \times 10^8 \times 0.01} $$

$$ C = \dfrac{0.5}{37500} $$

$$ \boxed{\, C = 13.33 \text{ }\mu\text{F} \,} $$

**Practical choice:** A standard $22 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, 25 V low-ESR electrolytic capacitor is recommended for design margin.

(Marks: duty 1, $L$ 3, $C$ 2, practical 1)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **Boost Converter** in CCM. Derive the expression for the output voltage and the critical value of inductance for continuous conduction. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** With a block diagram, describe the major functional blocks of a **closed-loop SMPS** and explain the role of the **optocoupler + TL431 feedback** in achieving voltage regulation. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Boost Converter

**Working Principle:**

- **Mode 1 (Switch ON):** Current builds up in inductor $L$ from $V_{in}$. The diode is reverse-biased. Output capacitor $C$ alone supplies the load.
- **Mode 2 (Switch OFF):** The inductor releases its stored energy. The inductor voltage adds to $V_{in}$ and forward-biases the diode, charging $C$ and feeding the load.

**Derivation (volt-second balance):**

$$ V_{in} \cdot D T_s + (V_{in} - V_o)(1-D) T_s = 0 $$

$$ V_{in} D + V_{in}(1-D) - V_o(1-D) = 0 $$

$$ V_{in} - V_o(1-D) = 0 $$

$$ \boxed{\, V_o = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1-D} \,} $$

**Critical Inductance:**

For CCM, the minimum inductor current must equal zero at the boundary:

$$ L_{crit} = \dfrac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s} $$

(Marks: working 2, derivation 3, critical $L$ 2)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Closed-Loop SMPS Block Diagram

**Major Functional Blocks (recap):**

1. **Input Rectifier + Filter** — Raw DC.
2. **PWM Controller** — Heart of regulation.
3. **Power Stage** — Switching device + magnetics.
4. **Output Filter** — Smooth DC.
5. **Feedback Network** — Optocoupler + TL431.
6. **Error Amplifier** — Inside the PWM controller.

**Role of Optocoupler + TL431:**

The **TL431** is a programmable shunt reference (2.5 V). It is connected as a comparator at the output: its reference pin samples a divider from $V_o$. When $V_o$ rises, the cathode current of the TL431 increases, which forward-biases the LED inside the **optocoupler (e.g., PC817)**. The optocoupler's phototransistor pulls the COMP pin of the PWM controller *down*, *reducing the duty cycle* $D$ and therefore lowering $V_o$. The reverse happens when $V_o$ falls. This creates a **negative-feedback closed loop**.

**The optocoupler also provides galvanic isolation** between the hazardous AC-mains side and the safe secondary side — a critical safety requirement mandated by IEC 60950 / IEC 62368.

(Marks: blocks 3, TL431 working 2, optocoupler isolation 2)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong polarity in Buck-Boost derivation** — students forget the *inverted* output polarity and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing PFM with PWM** — Pulse-Frequency Modulation varies $f_s$; Pulse-Width Modulation keeps $f_s$ constant. Most KTU lab SMPS units use PWM.
> 3. **Forgetting the *isolated* nature of Flyback** — losing 1 mark on the *why a transformer* question.
> 4. **Wrong unit on $L$** — writing $L = 500$ instead of $500 \text{ }\mu\text{H}$. Always carry the unit.
> 5. **Writing CCM conditions incorrectly** — the condition is $I_L \geq 0$ *throughout* $T_s$, not "average greater than zero."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print and keep for the night before the exam):**

- **SMPS** = regulated DC supply using a high-frequency switch + magnetic energy storage.
- **Linear vs SMPS:** Linear $\eta \leq 50\%$, SMPS $\eta \geq 80\%$.
- **Buck (step-down):** $V_o = D V_{in}$.
- **Boost (step-up):** $V_o = V_{in} / (1-D)$.
- **Buck-Boost (step-up/down, inverted):** $V_o = -D V_{in} / (1-D)$.
- **Flyback (isolated):** $V_o = D V_{in} (N_s / N_p) / (1-D)$.
- **CCM condition:** $I_L \geq 0$ throughout the switching period.
- **DCM condition:** $I_L = 0$ for some part of $T_s$ — usually at light loads.
- **Switching loss** scales linearly with $f_s$; **core loss** scales with $f_s^{\alpha}$ ($\alpha \approx 1.2 \rightarrow 1.5$). Trade-off $f_s = 25 \text{ kHz} \rightarrow 200 \text{ kHz}$ typical.
- **Volt-second balance** is the *fundamental analysis tool* — always state it explicitly in derivations.
- **PWM controllers to remember:** SG3525, UC3842, TL494, NE555.
- **Feedback isolation** uses optocoupler (PC817) + TL431.
- **Lab safety:** AC mains side is lethal; never probe the DC link while powered.
- **EMI filter** at input, **snubber** across the switch, and **freewheeling diode** with low reverse-recovery time are mandatory for reliable operation.
- **Hard-switched vs soft-switched** — KTU lab realization uses hard-switching (PWM); advanced SMPS use ZVS/ZCS for higher efficiency.
- **Always** report $V_o$, $I_o$, efficiency $\eta$, and the **measured vs theoretical duty cycle** in the lab record.

---

**End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Realization of Simple SMPS**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) — Outcome-Based Education.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC Motor Speed Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAEL607 - POWER ELECTRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** DC Motor Speed Control # ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **DC Motor Speed Control** in the context of Power Electronics Lab refers to the regulated variation of the rotational speed (N, in **RPM**) of a DC machine by precisely controlling the **armature terminal voltage (Va)** and/or the **field flux (Φ)** using solid-state switching power converters such as **DC-DC Choppers** (Buck/Step-down), **Phase-Controlled Rectifiers** (Semi-converters and Full-converters), and **PWM Inverters**. The fundamental governing speed equation of a separately excited DC motor is: $$N = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}$$ where **N** is the speed in RPM, **Va** is the applied armature voltage, **Ia** is the armature current, **Ra** is the armature resistance, **Φ** is the field flux per pole, and **k** is the machine construction constant. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment focuses specifically on **Chopper-Fed DC Motor Speed Control** using an IGBT/MOSFET-based step-down (Buck) chopper with a varying **duty cycle (D)**. The student must record the motor's no-load speed, plot the speed–armature voltage characteristic, and verify the inverse relationship between duty cycle and armature RMS voltage. ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy) Imagine driving a car on a highway. The speedometer reading is your **motor speed (N)**, the accelerator pedal controls the **fuel delivery (armature voltage Va)**, and the gear system alters the **engine's torque band (field flux Φ)**. Pressing the accelerator harder (more Va) makes the car go faster — but if you simultaneously shift to a higher gear (reduce Φ), the engine revs higher for the same power, increasing speed further. In a **Power Electronics Lab**, instead of a mechanical accelerator, we use an electronic "accelerator" — a **chopper circuit** — that rapidly switches the DC supply ON and OFF at high frequency (typically **1 kHz to 20 kHz**). By adjusting the **ON-time (Ton)** within a fixed switching period (T), we effectively "feather" the average voltage reaching the motor. This average voltage is what the motor "sees" and responds to, allowing smooth, stepless speed control from near zero to rated speed. > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary:** A chopper is an ultra-fast electronic switch that flicks the DC supply on and off thousands of times per second. The longer it stays "ON" per cycle, the higher the average voltage and the faster the motor spins. The shorter the "ON" time, the slower the motor. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit | |---|---|---| | Switching Frequency of Chopper | fs | **1 kHz – 20 kHz** (typical **2 kHz**) | | Switching Period | T = 1/fs | **0.5 ms** (for 2 kHz) | | Duty Cycle | D = Ton/T | **0 to 1** (dimensionless) | | Armature Resistance | Ra | **0.5 Ω – 5 Ω** (small rating motors) | | Field Flux | Φ | **Weber (Wb)** | | Speed | N | **RPM (Revolutions per Minute)** | | Back EMF Constant | Kb = Kt | **V·s/rad** | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always quote the duty cycle formula **D = Ton/T** in your answer. Examiners specifically check this exact form in **Part A (3-mark)** questions. --- # ⚡ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Methods of Speed Control — Conceptual Hierarchy A separately excited DC motor offers **three classical methods** of speed control, each with a different physical mechanism: 1. **Armature Voltage Control (Va method)** — Below base speed 2. **Field Flux Control (Φ method)** — Above base speed 3. **Combined (Va + Φ) Control** — Below AND above base speed (Constant HP region) In a Power Electronics Lab, **Method 1 (Armature Voltage Control via Chopper)** is the most commonly implemented experiment. ### 2.1.1 Why Use a Chopper and Not a Rheostat? A traditional **rheostatic control** (using a variable resistor) wastes significant power as **I²R heat** in the resistor. The chopper, by contrast, uses a fully-ON or fully-OFF semiconductor switch that dissipates negligible power (since either voltage across it is ~0 or current through it is ~0). This makes chopper-based control highly **efficient (>90%)** and **compact**. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Hook:** The chopper acts as a "DC Transformer." Just as an AC transformer steps voltage up or down using magnetic coupling, a chopper "steps down" the average DC voltage using time-ratio control. ## 2.2 The Step-Down (Buck) Chopper — Operating Principle A **step-down chopper** consists of a controlled switch (IGBT/MOSFET **T**), a freewheeling diode **Df**, and an inductive load (the DC motor armature, which has inductance **La** and resistance **Ra**). The input is a fixed DC source **Vs**. ### Operating Modes: **Mode 1: Switch T is ON (Duration = Ton = D·T)** - The supply Vs is connected directly across the load. - The load current **ia(t)** rises exponentially with time constant τ = La/Ra. - The freewheeling diode is reverse-biased and OFF. - The armature sees voltage = Vs. **Mode 2: Switch T is OFF (Duration = Toff = (1−D)·T)** - The supply is disconnected. - The inductive armature current cannot change instantaneously, so it freewheels through the diode **Df**. - The load voltage = 0 (diode drop is negligible). - The current decays exponentially. ## 2.3 Average Output Voltage Derivation The **average load voltage (Vo)** seen by the motor armature is the area under the voltage pulse divided by the total period: $$V_o = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T_{on}} V_s \, dt = V_s \cdot \frac{T_{on}}{T} = V_s \cdot D$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Equation:** Vo = D · Vs. This is the single most important formula for the entire experiment. Memorize it. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | S.No. | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_o = D \cdot V_s$ | Average output voltage of step-down chopper | **Volts (V)** | | 2 | $D = \frac{T_{on}}{T}$ | Duty Cycle definition | dimensionless | | 3 | $T = T_{on} + T_{off} = \frac{1}{f_s}$ | Total switching period | seconds (s) | | 4 | $N = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_b \Phi}$ | DC motor speed equation | **RPM** | | 5 | $V_{a(\text{avg})} = K_b \Phi \omega + I_a R_a$ | Armature voltage equation (back EMF form) | Volts (V) | | 6 | $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular speed conversion from RPM | rad/s | | 7 | $I_{a(\text{avg})} = \frac{V_s - V_{a(\text{avg})}}{R_a}$ | Average armature current (no-load approximation) | Amps (A) | | 8 | $I_{a(\text{rms})} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T i_a^2(t) \, dt}$ | RMS armature current (accounts for ripple) | Amps (A) | | 9 | $T_{\text{torque}} = K_t \Phi I_a$ | Electromagnetic torque developed | N·m | | 10 | $P_{\text{out}} = T_{\text{torque}} \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical output power | Watts (W) | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Pitfall Avoidance:** The absolute value $\vert x \vert$ must NEVER be written with vertical bars inside markdown tables. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Chopper-controlled DC motor drives are ubiquitous in modern industry: - **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** Traction motor speed control in older EVs and forklifts. - **Electric Trains / Trams (DC Traction):** Chopper-based cam control replaced rheostatic control in metros. - **Robotics:** Precise servo-positioning with DC motors. - **Cranes & Hoists:** Smooth lifting and lowering control. - **Paper Mills & Rolling Mills:** Tension and speed control of rollers. - **Battery-Powered Tools:** Cordless drills, screwdrivers. > [!NOTE] > **Industry Note:** Modern industrial drives have largely shifted to **VFDs (Variable Frequency Drives)** for AC motors because AC motors are cheaper and more robust. However, DC chopper drives are still preferred where **high starting torque, precise speed control, and quick dynamic response** are critical (e.g., elevators, steel rolling). --- # ⚡ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Average Output Voltage of a Step-Down Chopper **Given:** A step-down chopper fed from a constant DC source Vs feeds a DC motor armature. The chopper switch operates with period T and is ON for a duration Ton = D·T. **To Prove:** The average voltage across the motor armature equals D·Vs. ### Step 1: Define the voltage waveform across the load During the interval **0 ≤ t < Ton**, the switch T is ON. The freewheeling diode Df is reverse-biased because the load voltage tries to go negative, but the diode clamps it. The voltage across the motor armature is: $$v_o(t) = V_s \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \le t < T_{on}$$ During the interval **Ton ≤ t < T**, the switch T is OFF. The motor armature current freewheels through Df. The voltage across the motor armature equals the diode forward drop (taken as 0 V for an ideal diode): $$v_o(t) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad T_{on} \le t < T$$ ### Step 2: Set up the average value integral The average value of a periodic waveform vo(t) over one period T is: $$V_o = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v_o(t) \, dt$$ ### Step 3: Substitute the piecewise waveform Split the integral into the ON interval and the OFF interval: $$V_o = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_0^{T_{on}} V_s \, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0 \, dt \right]$$ ### Step 4: Evaluate the first integral The first integral is simply Vs multiplied by the duration of the ON interval: $$\int_0^{T_{on}} V_s \, dt = V_s \cdot T_{on}$$ The second integral is zero because the integrand is zero. ### Step 5: Substitute back and simplify $$V_o = \frac{1}{T} \cdot V_s \cdot T_{on} = V_s \cdot \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$ ### Step 6: Introduce duty cycle D By definition, the duty cycle is: $$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$ Therefore, the average output voltage becomes: $$\boxed{V_o = D \cdot V_s}$$ This is the **master equation** of the buck chopper. ∎ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Speed of DC Motor as a Function of Duty Cycle **Given:** A separately excited DC motor with armature resistance Ra, back EMF constant Kb, and constant field flux Φ is fed by a step-down chopper with supply voltage Vs and duty cycle D. **To Find:** The relationship between motor speed N and duty cycle D. ### Step 1: Write the armature circuit KVL equation The voltage applied to the armature terminals is the average chopper output voltage Vo. By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the armature loop: $$V_o = E_b + I_a R_a$$ where Eb is the back EMF (counter-EMF) of the motor and Ia is the armature current. ### Step 2: Express the back EMF in terms of angular speed The back EMF generated in a DC motor is directly proportional to the field flux and the angular velocity: $$E_b = K_b \Phi \omega$$ where ω is the angular speed in **rad/s**. ### Step 3: Substitute Vo from the chopper equation From Section 3.1, the average voltage applied to the motor is: $$V_o = D \cdot V_s$$ Therefore: $$D \cdot V_s = K_b \Phi \omega + I_a R_a$$ ### Step 4: Solve for angular speed ω Rearrange to isolate ω: $$K_b \Phi \omega = D \cdot V_s - I_a R_a$$ $$\omega = \frac{D \cdot V_s - I_a R_a}{K_b \Phi}$$ ### Step 5: Convert angular speed to RPM Since 1 revolution = 2π radians and 1 minute = 60 seconds: $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$ Substitute into the speed equation: $$\frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{D \cdot V_s - I_a R_a}{K_b \Phi}$$ ### Step 6: Solve for N $$N = \frac{60 \cdot (D \cdot V_s - I_a R_a)}{2\pi \cdot K_b \Phi}$$ $$\boxed{N = \frac{D \cdot V_s - I_a R_a}{K_b \Phi} \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi}}$$ This shows that **speed N is a linear function of duty cycle D**, with a small offset due to the armature resistance drop. Under no-load conditions, Ia is very small, and the relationship is approximately **linear**: **N ≈ (60 / 2π Kb Φ) · D · Vs**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examination Note:** For a 7-mark derivation in Part B, you must show all six steps above. Examiners specifically check Step 4 (isolating ω) and Step 5 (rad/s to RPM conversion) for partial marking. --- ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A 220 V, 10 A, 1500 RPM separately excited DC motor has armature resistance Ra = 0.5 Ω and field flux Φ = 0.05 Wb. The motor is fed by a step-down chopper from a 220 V DC source. Compute the motor speed when the chopper duty cycle is **D = 0.6** and the armature current is **Ia = 8 A**. ### Step 1: Compute average armature voltage $$V_a = D \cdot V_s = 0.6 \times 220 = 132 \, \text{V}$$ ### Step 2: Compute back EMF constant Kb from rated conditions At rated load: Vrated = 220 V, Irated = 10 A, Nrated = 1500 RPM. First, compute back EMF at rated condition: $$E_{b,\text{rated}} = V_{\text{rated}} - I_{\text{rated}} R_a = 220 - 10 \times 0.5 = 215 \, \text{V}$$ Compute rated angular speed: $$\omega_{\text{rated}} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \, \text{rad/s}$$ Compute Kb: $$K_b = \frac{E_{b,\text{rated}}}{\Phi \cdot \omega_{\text{rated}}} = \frac{215}{0.05 \times 157.08} = \frac{215}{7.854} = 27.37 \, \text{V·s/rad}$$ ### Step 3: Compute new back EMF at the given operating point $$E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 132 - 8 \times 0.5 = 132 - 4 = 128 \, \text{V}$$ ### Step 4: Compute new angular speed $$\omega = \frac{E_b}{K_b \Phi} = \frac{128}{27.37 \times 0.05} = \frac{128}{1.3685} = 93.53 \, \text{rad/s}$$ ### Step 5: Convert to RPM $$N = \frac{\omega \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{93.53 \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{5611.8}{6.2832} \approx 893.2 \, \text{RPM}$$ ### Final Answer: $$\boxed{N \approx 893 \, \text{RPM at } D = 0.6}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** The speed is reduced from 1500 RPM to 893 RPM when the average armature voltage is reduced from 220 V to 132 V. The reduction ratio (893/1500 ≈ 0.595) is close to the duty cycle (0.6), confirming the **linear D vs N relationship** for low armature resistance motors. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Speed Control Methods | Control Method | Speed Range | Direction | Power Electronics Implementation | Efficiency | |---|---|---|---|---| | Armature Voltage (Chopper) | **0 to Nbase** (Below base) | Forward only or Forward + Reverse (4-quadrant chopper) | Buck / Full-bridge chopper | **> 90%** | | Field Flux (Chopper on field) | **Nbase to Nmax** (Above base) | Forward only | Buck chopper on field winding | **> 90%** | | Semi-converter (1-Q) | Below base, **Forward motoring only** | Unidirectional | 1-Thyristor controlled bridge | ~85% | | Full-converter (2-Q) | Below base, **Forward + Reverse motoring** | Bidirectional | 2-Thyristor bridges in anti-parallel | ~85% | | Dual Converter (4-Q) | **Full four-quadrant** operation | Forward/Reverse motoring + Forward/Reverse braking | Two full-converters in anti-parallel | ~80% | | Ward-Leonard (Rotary) | Full range | Smooth bidirectional | Motor-Generator set | **~70%** (older method) | > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Favourite:** Be ready to draw a comparison between **chopper control** and **converter control** (semi-converter vs full-converter) in a 7-mark question. --- # ⚡ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Step-Down Chopper Circuit with DC Motor Load (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD DCsupply["DC SOURCE Vs = 220 V"] --> ChopperSwitch["CHOPPER SWITCH T (IGBT or MOSFET)"] ChopperSwitch -- "Turn ON: 0 to Ton" --> LoadPath["APPLY Vs ACROSS MOTOR"] ChopperSwitch -- "Turn OFF: Ton to T" --> DiodeOn["FREEWHEEL DIODE Df CONDUCTS"] DiodeOn --> LoadPath LoadPath --> Motor["DC MOTOR ARMATURE (La + Ra)"] Motor --> SpeedSensor["TACHOGENERATOR / ENCODER"] SpeedSensor --> Display["DIGITAL SPEED DISPLAY N in RPM"] ControlUnit["PWM CONTROL UNIT (DUTY CYCLE ADJUST)"] -. Gate Signal .-> ChopperSwitch ControlUnit -. Feedback .-> SpeedSensor ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Guide:** The solid arrows show the **power flow** path. The dotted arrows show the **control signal** path. The control unit adjusts the duty cycle D, which determines the average voltage Vo = D·Vs reaching the motor. ## 4.2 Waveform Sequence (Chopper Output and Motor Current) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TimeDomain["ONE COMPLETE SWITCHING PERIOD T = Ton + Toff"] direction LR Vswave["VOLTAGE WAVEFORM vo t
Vs from 0 to Ton
0 from Ton to T"] Iawave["ARMATURE CURRENT ia t
RISES during Ton
DECAYS during Toff"] Gwave["GATE PULSE TO IGBT
HIGH from 0 to Ton
LOW from Ton to T"] end Vswave --- Iawave Iawave --- Gwave ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Waveform Insight:** Notice the **ripple in armature current** during the OFF period. This ripple magnitude is determined by the inductance La. If La is too small, the current may become discontinuous, causing torque pulsations. This is why a **smoothing inductor** is often added in series with the armature in practical circuits. ## 4.3 Speed vs Duty Cycle Characteristic (Expected Experimental Plot) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["D = 0.0
N approx 0 RPM"] --> B["D = 0.25
N approx 380 RPM"] B --> C["D = 0.50
N approx 745 RPM"] C --> D["D = 0.75
N approx 1115 RPM"] D --> E["D = 1.00
N approx 1500 RPM"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Plot Interpretation:** This is a **linear, monotonically increasing** characteristic. The slope of the line is $\frac{60 \cdot V_s}{2\pi \cdot K_b \cdot \Phi}$ under no-load conditions. The small intercept (around 0–50 RPM) is due to armature resistance drop and friction. ## 4.4 System Block Diagram of the Complete Speed Control Setup ```mermaid flowchart TD Mains["230 V AC MAINS"] --> Rectifier["UNCONTROLLED DIODE BRIDGE RECTIFIER"] Rectifier --> Filter["LC FILTER CAPACITOR"] Filter --> DCbus["DC LINK BUS Vdc approx 310 V"] DCbus --> Chopper["STEP-DOWN CHOPPER IGBT"] Chopper --> Armature["DC MOTOR ARMATURE"] Armature --> Field["SEPARATE EXCITATION FIELD WINDING"] Field --> DCbus Chopper -. "PWM Gate Signal" .-> Controller["DSP or MICROCONTROLLER"] Controller -. "Reference Speed Nref" .-> Pot["POTENTIOMETER SPEED SETTING"] ``` > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight:** In many lab setups, the **DC bus is derived from AC mains through a diode bridge rectifier + filter capacitor**. This converts the AC mains to a fixed DC voltage (~310 V from 230 V AC), which is then chopped down to the required armature voltage level. This **AC-DC-DC cascade** is a common power electronic architecture. --- # ⚡ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q: Define duty cycle of a chopper. Write the expression for the average output voltage of a step-down chopper.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **duty cycle (D)** of a chopper is defined as the ratio of the ON-time (Ton) of the switching device to the total switching period (T): $$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$ It is a dimensionless quantity that can vary between 0 and 1. The **average output voltage (Vo)** of an ideal step-down (Buck) chopper fed from a DC source Vs is given by: $$V_o = D \cdot V_s = \frac{T_{on}}{T} \cdot V_s$$ **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + Formula (1) + Explanation (1). --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: List any three methods of speed control of a DC motor.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three classical methods of speed control of a separately excited DC motor are: 1. **Armature Voltage Control** — Va is varied while field flux Φ is kept constant. Speed below base speed. 2. **Field Flux Control** — Φ is varied (by varying field current) while Va is kept constant. Speed above base speed. 3. **Combined (Va + Φ) Control** — Both Va and Φ are varied. Provides speed control over the entire range. **Alternative methods:** Ward-Leonard system, DC chopper control, Phase-controlled rectifier control. **Mark Distribution:** Each method (1) × 3 = 3. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a step-down chopper used for DC motor speed control. Draw the relevant voltage and current waveforms. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the relationship between the motor speed N and the duty cycle D for a separately excited DC motor fed by a step-down chopper. A 220 V, 1500 RPM DC motor has armature resistance Ra = 1 Ω and constant field flux. Calculate the speed when the chopper operates at a duty cycle of 0.5 and the armature draws 10 A. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]: **Step 1: Circuit Description (2 marks)** A step-down chopper consists of: - A controlled switch **T** (IGBT/MOSFET) connected between the DC source Vs and the load. - A **freewheeling diode Df** connected across the load in reverse polarity. - The **DC motor armature** (Ra + La) as the load. **Step 2: Operation (3 marks)** - **Mode 1 (0 to Ton, Switch T ON):** The supply Vs is connected across the motor armature. The armature current ia rises exponentially. The freewheeling diode is reverse-biased. - **Mode 2 (Ton to T, Switch T OFF):** The supply is disconnected. Due to the inductive nature of the armature, the current cannot drop to zero instantly and instead freewheels through Df. The motor terminal voltage is 0 (ideal diode). **Step 3: Waveforms (2 marks)** The expected waveforms are: - Gate pulse to switch T: rectangular pulse, ON for Ton, OFF for Toff. - Output voltage vo(t): Vs during ON, 0 during OFF. - Armature current ia(t): exponentially rising during ON, exponentially decaying during OFF (with average value Ia,avg). #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]: **Step 1: Derive the speed equation (4 marks)** From the chopper analysis: Va = D · Vs = 0.5 × 220 = 110 V. The motor armature voltage equation: $$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$ $$E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 110 - 10 \times 1 = 100 \, \text{V}$$ At rated condition (D = 1, Vs = 220 V, Ia,rated = 10 A, Nrated = 1500 RPM): $$E_{b,\text{rated}} = 220 - 10 \times 1 = 210 \, \text{V}$$ Since Eb ∝ N (with constant Φ): $$\frac{N}{N_{\text{rated}}} = \frac{E_b}{E_{b,\text{rated}}}$$ **Step 2: Numerical Calculation (3 marks)** $$N = N_{\text{rated}} \times \frac{E_b}{E_{b,\text{rated}}} = 1500 \times \frac{100}{210}$$ $$N = 1500 \times 0.4762 = 714.3 \, \text{RPM}$$ $$\boxed{N \approx 714 \, \text{RPM at } D = 0.5}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Many students incorrectly use the **no-load speed approximation** ($N = D \cdot N_{\text{rated}}$) and write N = 750 RPM. The correct answer must account for the **armature resistance drop**, giving 714 RPM. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this oversight. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Compare armature voltage control method and field flux control method of DC motor speed control. Mention the relative advantages and disadvantages of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the closed-loop speed control scheme of a DC motor using a chopper. Discuss the role of the PI controller in maintaining constant speed under varying load. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]: **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Feature | Armature Voltage Control | Field Flux Control | |---|---|---| | Parameter varied | Va (with Φ constant) | Φ (with Va constant) | | Speed range | **0 to Nbase** (Below base) | **Nbase to Nmax** (Above base) | | Power Electronics | Step-down chopper on armature | Step-down chopper on field | | Torque capability | **Constant torque** region | **Constant power** region | | Speed at no load | Variable, controlled by D | Variable, controlled by If | | Stability | Inherently stable | Unstable for very high speeds (field weakening) | **Advantages of Armature Voltage Control (1 mark):** - Better speed regulation - Constant torque capability - Faster dynamic response **Disadvantages (1 mark):** - Requires higher current-rated chopper - Limited to speeds below base **Advantages of Field Flux Control:** Simple, lower current rating, can achieve speeds above base. **Disadvantages:** Loss of torque at high speeds, poor speed regulation. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]: **Block Diagram (3 marks):** The closed-loop speed control system consists of: 1. **Reference Speed (Nref)** — Set by the user via potentiometer. 2. **Error Generator** — Compares Nref with actual speed feedback Nact. 3. **PI Controller** — Generates the control signal based on error (P + I action). 4. **PWM Generator** — Converts control signal to variable duty cycle gate pulse. 5. **Chopper** — Switches the DC supply to motor armature at the given duty cycle. 6. **DC Motor** — The plant. 7. **Tachogenerator/Encoder** — Measures actual speed and feeds it back. **Explanation of PI Controller Role (4 marks):** When the load torque on the motor increases suddenly, the motor tends to slow down. The actual speed Nact decreases, the error (Nref − Nact) becomes positive, and the PI controller output increases. This raises the duty cycle D, which increases Va = D·Vs, supplying more current and torque to overcome the increased load. Once the load disturbance is removed, the controller readjusts the duty cycle to its previous value. - **P (Proportional) action:** Provides immediate correction. - **I (Integral) action:** Eliminates steady-state error (offset). - **D (Derivative) action** (optional): Provides damping and reduces overshoot. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall in Closed-Loop Questions:** Students often forget to mention the **negative feedback** nature of the loop. Always show the error signal as (Nref − Nact) and indicate the **subtraction** at the summing junction. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (General) > [!WARNING] > **Critical Mark-Loss Zones for DC Motor Speed Control Questions:** > 1. **Formula Sign Error:** Writing $N = \frac{V_a + I_a R_a}{K_b \Phi}$ instead of $N = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_b \Phi}$. Deduction: **2 marks**. > 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing rad/s and RPM in the same equation without conversion. Deduction: **1 mark**. > 3. **Missing Freewheeling Diode:** Drawing a chopper circuit without Df. Deduction: **1 mark**. > 4. **Skipping the Average Value Derivation:** Writing Vo = D·Vs without showing the integration. Deduction: **2 marks** (for 7-mark questions). > 5. **Confusing Buck and Boost:** Writing Vo = Vs/(1−D) for a step-down chopper. Deduction: **3 marks**. > 6. **No-Load vs Loaded Operation:** Using no-load speed formula when loaded conditions are given. Deduction: **1–2 marks**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Print this section and keep it beside you during revision.** - ✅ **Master Equation:** $V_o = D \cdot V_s$ — Average voltage of step-down chopper. - ✅ **Speed Equation:** $N = \frac{60(V_a - I_a R_a)}{2\pi K_b \Phi}$ — Fundamental motor speed formula. - ✅ **Duty Cycle:** $D = \frac{T_{on}}{T}$, range **0 to 1**. - ✅ **Switching Period:** $T = \frac{1}{f_s}$; typical $f_s$ = 1–20 kHz. - ✅ **Two Operating Modes:** Switch ON (Vo = Vs) and Switch OFF (Vo = 0, freewheeling through diode). - ✅ **Freewheeling Diode Role:** Provides a path for inductive armature current during the OFF period, preventing voltage spikes. - ✅ **Three Speed Control Methods:** Armature voltage, Field flux, Combined. - ✅ **Constant Torque Region:** Below base speed — armature voltage control. - ✅ **Constant Power Region:** Above base speed — field flux control. - ✅ **D vs N Relationship:** Approximately linear under no-load; small offset under load. - ✅ **Back EMF:** $E_b = K_b \Phi \omega$ — proportional to speed and flux. - ✅ **Closed-Loop Components:** Reference → Error → PI Controller → PWM → Chopper → Motor → Tachogenerator → Feedback. - ✅ **PI Controller:** P-action for fast response, I-action for zero steady-state error. - ✅ **Advantages of Chopper Control:** High efficiency (>90%), compact, fast response, no commutation issues. - ✅ **Disadvantages of Chopper Control:** Harmonics in armature current, requires freewheeling diode, switch stress during commutation. --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — DC Motor Speed Control.** > These notes are aligned to **PCAEL607 – Power Electronics Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)** and follow the **NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education** pattern. Students are advised to also refer to the **KTU Virtual Lab Power Electronics portal** for simulation-based learning of the chopper-fed DC motor drive experiment.
